output: Thank you, Mr. President. Mr. President, Madam Vice-President, the Commission gives the impression of acting like a distracted student rushing to catch up on lost time before handing in their exam paper.
Certainly, the social package exists, but it is incomplete and can only be regarded as a step toward the Commission's consideration of social issues.

The President of the Commission failed to heed the warning issued by citizens in France, the Netherlands, and Ireland, a warning that would unfortunately have been identical elsewhere had there been a popular vote.
These elections are reminders to get back on track.
If Europeans want more Europe, it is to build a social project, not merely to adopt yet more technical directives.
Regulation concerning the internal market is indeed necessary, but it is by no means the essential issue.
The 2009 program, like its predecessors, will miss the point.
Citizens do not expect the Commission merely to complete, let alone simplify, the legislation.
They expect the Commission, should it live up to the task assigned to it by the treaties, to be the locomotive, the think tank of the Union.
It is not enough for its president to be wise and silent, standing at attention behind the President of the Council.
We expect the Commission to act as a key player in reflecting on the nature of the societal project we need,
But what is the social project behind this program?
The 2009 program contains no major errors, but it is not the program our fellow citizens need.
Mr. President, it will be the new College that assumes its responsibilities.
I only hope he will be less deaf to the appeals of peoples than the current one.
Thank you, Mr. President.

of the high-level conference on Georgia, which had been proposed by the French presidency, and how the current EU presidency intends to uphold Georgia's territorial integrity, particularly as a country recognizes two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia.
But it's a pity that Mr. Barroso is not here, because in his speech he was not convincing, perhaps because he himself is not convinced by the proposals he has put forward to us.
I believe the financial and economic situation is far more serious than he has described it to us.
And even today, I believe those who follow the internet clearly see that the stock markets continue to collapse despite interventions.

For me, it is the collapse of the current system, a completely outdated system—the system that led to bankruptcy, that caused the earth to be entirely exploited and suffocated by greenhouse gas emissions, that triggered underdevelopment in the Global South, and that has caused underdevelopment in our cities as well.
I thank Madame President, Madame Commissioner. The current financial crisis clearly shows that when the issue is global, a global response is required.
And this is particularly true in the field of agriculture or in the food sector.
This is why, among other things, the World Trade Organization was established, which we agree upon, and which seeks to implement globally applicable rules.

It turns out that this does not quite work as we would like, because we are faced with the following problem. Two things must be entrusted: the necessary free trade, on which everyone agrees, and the equally necessary protections.
And protecting national economies or agriculture does not mean protectionism.
To achieve this, the path we have followed for the past 60 years, since GATT, has been an unbalanced one, based on the reduction of customs duties with the objective of their eventual elimination.
It happens that technically, the reduction of customs duties
It's difficult. The proof is a whole series of mathematical formulas: the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the most well-known one, the Swiss formula.
It doesn't really work because a product is not unitary
It comprises numerous elements with rules originating from very different sources, and technically reducing customs duties is not as straightforward as one might think.
We are therefore in a technical deadlock, and politically, we attempt to emerge from this deadlock through negotiations.

Now, it happens that technologically, scientists have developed a new customs technology: the technology of deductible duties, deductible by the exporter from the economic savings of the importing country.
In practice, this deductible customs duty is transformed into a customs credit equal to the amount of the customs duty that the exporter incurred in the importing country.
This customs credit has three characteristics: it is refundable, negotiable, and eligible for discounting.
It is refundable at the moment the exporter purchases from the importer, at which point the exporter may deduct the duty it has paid.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company that has paid 100 in customs duties has nothing to purchase from the importer, it will negotiate its customs credit on the stock market or at a bank.
And it is beneficial if we wish to assist developing countries, as the importer may offer a customs credit amount exceeding the amount of customs duty.
With this technique, we create an international trade currency whose money supply equals the amount of existing customs duties.
For example, the European Union would provide 13 billion euros of international currency in the commercial sector.

Thank you, Mr. President. The Russo-Georgian conflict and the financial crisis provided the European Union with the opportunity to assert itself on the world stage as a political actor in its own right and of the highest order.
Thanks to your determination, Mr. President of the Council, Europe has succeeded in uniting and finding coordinated, effective, and swift responses.
Faced with crises, Europe proves that it exists and demonstrates its added value.
In the matter of immigration, for example, individual self-interest was no longer acceptable.
The adoption by the European Council of the migration and asylum pact, initiated by the French presidency, represents a significant advancement.
On the fight against climate change, Europe must set concrete goals and show the way to its global partners.

You have already expressed yourself on this matter, Mr. President of the Council, but we wish this agreement to be as balanced as possible, as it must take into account the economic situation of our countries, which is currently highly destabilized.
but also raise the question of the kind of development we will choose for the future.
To meet the challenges ahead, the European Union needs the Treaty of Lisbon more than ever.
We understand the concerns of our Irish friends, but the status quo is not an option.
Ireland must offer us a solution at the December European Council. Many of us are demanding this.
In recent weeks, we have proudly observed a strong, politically united Europe, standing together in adversity, resolute in its dealings with partners, and listened to on the international stage.
We wish that this new European mindset becomes the norm and endures
The Treaty of Lisbon is the best solution we have at our disposal to achieve this.

Thank you, Madam President. Mr. Minister, I was in Tbilisi when President Sarkozy came to negotiate the peace plan. Of course, I pay tribute to him for acting swiftly and successfully bringing this war to an end.
But this war is, to some extent, our failure. For 14 years, we have been hesitant. We stood silent as provocations escalated in the separatist regions.

And this war, certainly, has awakened Europe, reminding it of its responsibilities. But the fire continues to smolder in the Caucasus. And we must do everything possible to finally resolve the frozen conflicts, for the security of all Europe.
And I am also aware, Mr. Minister, of the divisions among European countries regarding Georgia's entry into NATO. Personally, I am opposed to it.
And I will make you a proposal. I will ask the European Union to propose the status of neutrality for these Caucasus countries.
Only the status of neutrality will be able to ease tensions with Russia and definitively prevent this subregion from further conflicts.
This status will ensure the security of these young democracies and contribute to our own security.
Mr. Prime Minister, over the past three days, the European Development Days have taken place, during which the severe food crisis affecting the poorest countries has been widely discussed.
All interventions emphasized the absolute necessity for developing countries to develop their own agriculture by optimally utilizing their land and natural resources.

In passing, the lyrical outbursts of those who called yesterday and still call today for reducing the state's intervention and public spending, for unrestrained liberalization, privatization, and the relegation of agriculture as
a quasi prehistoric activity, this sudden reversal therefore favoring massive public investments in agricultural production and food sovereignty, all of it tinged with both absurdity and scandal.
The future of agriculture in developing countries is directly linked to the debate we are having today.

Unfortunately, I do not feel that this assessment of the CAP's health, initiated before the peak of the food riots and the disastrous market upheavals, takes into account the situation of southern peasant communities or fully addresses the serious risks we face today—ecological, social, economic, and financial.

I therefore deeply regret that the strong relationship between farmers from the North and the South is so little addressed, both in the Commission's proposals and in the Parliament's report. The undermining of the promised one billion euros underscores just how far the journey still is.
Thank you, Mr. President. The report we are about to vote on tomorrow marks the culmination of a year of work and negotiations devoted to the future of the Common Agricultural Policy and our farmers.
Indeed, it was last 20 November that Mrs. Fischer-Bowle came to present her proposals regarding the health check-up report.
Following Lutz Goebbels' tables, here comes the report by Capula-Santo on the Commission's proposal.
We should congratulate our two colleagues for their excellent work.
The European Parliament's Committee on Agriculture has managed to remain firm in the face of the Commission's initial proposals, which we considered far too liberal.
I will remain vigilant with regard to the milk sector and, in particular, the measures preceding the abandonment of quotas.

I am particularly thinking of mountain dairy producers, who must not become the poor relations of this reform, and we must ensure they are not left behind, as they will require greater support than others.
I don't wish to play the Cassandra, but how can one imagine the French dairy industry concentrated within a single region?
I therefore welcome with goodwill the proposal to establish a milk fund, as well as the one suggesting that milk quota increases be assessed as of 2010 according to market developments.

The calculation of the progressive adjustment made by the Agriculture Commission appears to be correct. It would enable us to strengthen territorial development, rural vitality, and sustainably preserve biodiversity.
I also commend the clarification of eco-conditionality, whose implementation had previously been a real headache for farmers.

I also wish for us to maintain the amount of the tobacco fund, which enables us to preserve our small farms across Europe, as well as a significant workforce in a region where nothing else is viable, and which helps us avoid importing products.
Finally, Mr. Minister, we greatly appreciated your commitment throughout these negotiations.
You have shown great patience and great determination, and you have not spared your time in vain.
It was a genuine pleasure working alongside you.
You have been very effective with us.
I know we can rely on you.
First of all, thank you for the work completed and for the conclusions and recommendations proposed in the health check of the CAP contained in Mr. Caboulos-Santos's report.
Agriculture is indeed an economic sector that cannot respond to market signals alone.
Its activity inevitably affects food self-sufficiency, but also, all too often, land use and employment.
The elimination of market instruments would be a serious blow to our agriculture.

Decoupling, for example, poses a genuine challenge for certain livestock regions, particularly those raising dairy cows and beef cattle, and I welcome having heard Mrs. Fischer-Boll address this issue earlier.
Moreover, it is clear that strictly maintaining historical references would severely hinder any possibility of reform.
Nevertheless, I still believe that the issue of the second pillar of the CAP is essential, as rural areas need to rely on agricultural activity to develop employment in rural regions.
And thus one must consider the issue of the second foot without, of course, amputating the first.
Given that Minister Jouyens is about to leave us, I believe this Parliament should thank him.
He was one of the most diligent ministers we have ever known.
I wish him good luck.

Yes, Madam President, I voted in favor of the Perriche report regarding the integration of OCM20 into the old Munich framework, but I did so with great reluctance, as I believe this single OCM does not constitute a simplification and does not enhance transparency.
It will make life more complicated for viticulturists and the entire wine industry.
Last night, the commissioner tried to reassure us
I hope the Commission will keep its promise and that, above all, the profession will continue to be adequately represented on the advisory committee, as has been the case since the first OCM 20.

Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, our group enthusiastically supports the proposal for the Small Business Act that the Commission presented to us last June.

Indeed, since the 2000s, initiatives have been undertaken regarding SMEs, and I particularly think of the excellent work by our colleague Dominique Blasto on the SME charter. These initiatives were appreciated but considered insufficient because they often remained little more than pious wishes.
Today, we must go further
The severe economic crisis our countries are experiencing, whose consequences are particularly concerning for SMEs, invites us to do so, if further invitation were needed.
In this regard, the Small Business Act represents a new approach, decidedly proactive.
In a few weeks, we will examine Mrs. Herzog's report on the Commission's communication. But today, through the submission of this oral question, we have wished to address the Council.

Mr. Minister, we have noted with interest the conclusions adopted yesterday by the Competitiveness Council, and we would like to know whether the European Council, at its meeting on 11 and 12 December next,

will clearly articulate its conclusions and provide strong impetus to ensure the overarching guiding principle "think small first" is swiftly incorporated into the regulatory frameworks of the Member States.
We wish for the Council to commit to the principle of the action plan attached, resulting in concrete measures.
In this regard, and within the limited time available to me, I will emphasize three directions in which we need binding impulses.
the impact assessments that should be systematic, and regular monitoring with parliamentary oversight of the implementation of the Small Business Act in the Member States,
access to public procurement
The code of conduct has no binding force. We must, together, be more imaginative and innovative, and propose concrete, appropriate measures that genuinely open up markets to SMEs.
Finally, access to financing, more indispensable than ever in the current context.
Your proposals are good, Mr. Minister. They must also include support for supply.
There is an emergency
Please ensure us of the Council's intention.

Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, we all know that SMEs are the backbone of the European Union, since they employ more than 70% of the EU's workforce and account for 99% of European enterprises.

But as already mentioned, these millions of small and medium-sized enterprises are now troubled and threatened by the financial crisis, as it undermines household purchasing power and confidence, and affects order books.

It is therefore the cash flow of SMEs that is at risk, and accordingly, efforts must be made to support their activities by ensuring that banks guarantee effective access to the financing necessary for the continuity of their operations.

This is precisely the issue addressed in the conclusions of the Competitiveness Council of 1 December, which the Minister has mentioned, as well as in the recovery plan approved by the European Ministers of Finance.
Making the needs of SMEs a permanent political priority for the Union is essential. And I would like to commend the action plan in support of the Small Business Act for Europe.
It indeed reflects the intention to ensure the effective implementation of the guiding principle, giving priority to small ones first, that is, to European SMEs.
However, as the Commissioner has said, and as we all maintain, the Council must genuinely engage with the other European institutions.
European. For this principle to now become effective, it is necessary to go beyond the declaration; it is necessary to implement it across all European policies and programs.
However, we can say that this action plan represents a genuine response to the urgency of the situation.
And, Mr. Minister, I would like to commend the determination of the French presidency of the Union, which enabled intentions to be swiftly translated into action.
Madam President, in office, Commissioner, I fully support the rapporteur, who is upholding the position we took during the first reading, a position rejected by the governments of the Member States.
It was urgent to bring our legislation on rest periods into line with the case law of the Court of Justice. This has now been accomplished.
The Cercas report provides balanced and employee-protective responses.
All time spent on duty is counted as working time,
The compensatory rest takes place immediately after the service period.
It is a matter of common sense to ensure decent working conditions, particularly for medical professions.
However, the reform of the Working Time Directive also provides us with the opportunity to advance our European social law by abolishing the individual opt-out.
The Cercas report seizes this opportunity and proposes a gradual elimination of any possibility to derogate from the maximum legal working time.
One must face reality squarely
It is a joke to claim that the employee is in an equal position vis-à-vis their employer and can refuse what is offered.
Dear colleagues, we must clearly show the government of the member states that the text it wishes to impose on us is unacceptable.

And amidst the chorus of praise that will surely soon soothe the ears of the French government, I believe it is essential to first focus on the employees who will be required to work ever more, without truly having a choice, just as those who will be asked tomorrow to work on Sundays in France.
Indeed, it is clearly in order to open up this possibility that the French government changed its position on individual opt-out at the Council.

Dear colleagues, let us listen to the workers and strive to answer their call. If we do not wish the negative referendums in France, the Netherlands, and Ireland to be followed by many more, undermining an European Union that makes them feel its policies do not address their daily concerns. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. This was a very successful French presidency.
There were very few dissenting voices in refusing to agree.
The 27 have set aside their differences to reach a series of decisions that have been exemplary.
On the climate-energy package, Europe is taking a first step, and in this regard, that first step is immense.
She now has the means to assume her environmental responsibility without compromising her economy.
It can now take the lead in this matter, guiding other continents toward sustainable development.

Our Irish friends will once again vote on the Lisbon Treaty with all the cards on the table, since practically all the other members will have definitively expressed their positions.
Current crises demonstrate that the Union more than ever needs the best institutional tools to be effective.
The European Council adopted a large-scale, coordinated economic recovery plan, based on long-term productive investment and targeted support for the industrial sectors most severely affected,
President Sarkozy raised the issue of reducing certain VAT rates, and I fully share his viewpoint.
Let us hope that the ministers of economics and finance will reach an agreement in March that seemed impossible to the head of state and government during the previous weekend.
The challenges facing Europeans provide the Union with the opportunity to reveal itself to them as more united, more responsive, and more committed to protecting them.
We are grateful to the French presidency for having injected this new momentum
We wish, like Nicolas Sarkozy, never again to have to endure the late-night meetings of yesterday that dragged on until 4 a.m. just to negotiate three peanuts.
Over the past six months, Europe has indeed undergone a change in mindset, scope, and credibility.
We must thank the French presidency for this. We are fully aware that it is not entirely unrelated to them. Thank you, Mr. President.
The situation in Gaza is known to everyone.
What we are discussing here in Parliament is natural.

But we are asking the Security Council to take a stand. We are asking the relevant bodies to take a stand, the European Union to take a stand. And we, in Parliament, are discussing and do not wish to take a position.

I believe that, despite everything, given the urgency of the situation in Gaza, it is of utmost importance that this Parliament speak out and clearly state exactly what it wants in order to put an end to the massacres currently taking place in the Middle East.
I find unacceptable that this Parliament lacks the courage and clarity to adopt a resolution following our debate.
That is why our group requests that the decision of the Presidents' Conference be revised and that we conclude the debate we must address.
to take a stance on the situation in Gaza through a resolution that will clearly and firmly demonstrate this Parliament's majority position, so that the massacres in Gaza can stop.

We want a resolution. We must be aware that we bear a political responsibility for what is happening. And this political responsibility cannot merely be a discussion; it must be a resolution that clearly states what we want and what we condemn.
Mr. President, Commissioner, more than ever, the proposal for a directive that Parliament is about to vote on evokes in me a very mixed feeling.
It is true that the rapporteur, Mr. Cleanse, has carried out sustained work for many years and has negotiated skillfully, particularly during the Trilongue.

It is also true that the directive represents a number of significant advances toward a better functioning of the internal market and the collective management of financial assets, and has been welcomed as such by the European investment fund industry.

It remains true that debates and discussions within the Council and Parliament were never entirely free, since the outcome was already known in advance. For certain actors, it was absolutely essential to introduce the European passport for management companies.
On principle, there is nothing to object to. The passport holds up quite well. However, it is essential to ensure that the introduction of the passport for management companies
accompanied by the necessary safeguards, particularly regarding the supervision of funds, since it leads to a separation of functions across borders.
Moreover, I regret to note that the system implemented is not only complex, but also subject to divergent interpretations.
They may quickly encounter practical difficulties that will harm European industry, particularly in exporting goods to third countries.
I have fully acknowledged my position as a minority, yet I do not feel uncomfortable.
Under normal circumstances, I would have been tempted to say that only practice will allow us to draw definitive conclusions on this matter and thus determine
Who is right between those who trust the market participants' ability to adapt and those who adhere to a more cautious approach?
It is not impossible that success lies ahead. But on the other hand, nothing guarantees it either.
Moreover, the very recent news, particularly the Madoff scandal and its implications for the collective asset management industry, is not reassuring.
Sapthicism gives way to my unease
The investment fund industry will not be spared by the financial crisis; we are already observing this firsthand.
Fundamental challenges may be necessary within this context of crisis.
that in these contexts of deep crisis, making choices which dilute the responsibilities of actors or which presuppose perfect cooperation among supervisory authorities can truly appear incongruous.
I'm telling you as I truly believe: this erectile dysfunction is also that of a bygone era, prior to the crisis.
It is marked by a certain carelessness.
Indeed, the financial system is about to undergo a deep and lasting crisis of confidence. Large parts of its structure must be rethought.

Let us not forget, the primary function of UCITS is to protect the investor through risk diversification and the establishment of strict rules. By voting for this text in its current form, without truly knowing where we are headed, we are acting as though nothing has happened.
This autism portends nothing good; that is why I shall refrain, even though I have signed the compromise amendments negotiated with the Council.
Given the circumstances, the improvements introduced seem indeed of little consequence compared to the fundamental issues I have just explained.

Mr. President, although the regulation on animal reporting has been in effect since January 2007, it appears that Member States are not systematically complying by submitting the annual reports requested of them.
Moreover, a comprehensive analysis remains arbitrary, since many elements concerning the means implemented to carry out inspections are lacking?
Member of animal protection associations and member of the parliamentary group for animal welfare, this issue particularly concerns me.

Associations have been desperately fighting for decades until, finally in 2007, guidelines were established in this area, where it has become evident that Member States have displayed a certain laxity.
I would even go so far as to speak of a certain unwillingness. After all, we know that implementing control and inspection is by no means insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the breeding sites, we know the livestock transit routes. So, where is the problem?

I believe it is important in our time, when public opinion is increasingly, and rightly, attentive to animal welfare—whether in terms of breeding, slaughter, or transport conditions—that states respect these sentiments.

And since I have the floor, I would like to add that, with regard to transportation, it would be essential to examine and take into account, independently of transportation times, the local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a journey lasting several hours conducted in the Netherlands during spring bears no resemblance to the same duration of travel during peak summer in a country such as Greece.
Should nighttime transportation not be imposed in these final cases?
I thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

I am pleased that it is a Briton, our colleague Parish, who has submitted this text, asserting that Roman soldiers two thousand years ago were indeed right to go to Great Britain and bring amphorae of olive oil and wine.
But then two consequences must be drawn from it.

It is not very reasonable, symbolically, to locate the food agency in Helsinki. And it is unreasonable for the European Commission not to backtrack on the following points. First, restore aid for olive growers. Second, halt the amendments.

attacks being carried out against olive trees, particularly in Lousie and throughout Europe, halt the attacks being made against tuna fishermen in the Mediterranean, and the attacks being made against European vineyards through uprooting
I suggest that our Commissioner, our President, Mr. Barroso, organize a grand banquet in Portugal featuring sheep's cheese and Saint-Germain wine.
Thank you, Mr. President. Indeed, we have all advocated for the closure of Guantanamo, and we all welcome President Obama's decision to close this prison of shame.
Today, the question of accommodating Guantanamo detainees should not arise for us.
As Europeans, faithful to our shared values of defending human rights, we must accept our responsibilities, for it is our duty to welcome these former detainees.
And one could read, and here I am addressing the Council, in the press that some Member States were not very enthusiastic about this reception.
In this regard, I would like to issue a genuine appeal to the Council and, in particular, to the hesitant Member States, urging them to accept hosting these detainees on their territory.

And I would say to you, dear colleagues, that the worst outcome would be if the promising opportunity arising from the closure of Guantanamo were to stall simply because we failed to respond when Europe was called upon.

important because the European animal feed sector constitutes one of the most significant agricultural sectors, both in terms of production—representing half of the European Union's agricultural output, or 120 million tonnes—and turnover, amounting to approximately 50 billion euros.
The European Union indeed has 5 million farmers and 60 million households owning pets.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant regarding transparency in order to meet the expectations of farmers, as well as those of consumers.

The provisions outlined in this report have the advantage of imposing minimal costs on industries while delivering significant benefits to consumers, who are increasingly attentive to the quality of the products they purchase.

Adopting this report will help mitigate risks by ensuring improved product quality, better control, enhanced traceability, and greater information availability for the farmer, and thus, ultimately, for the consumer.
Today, with the growing volume of international exchanges, it is imperative to strengthen all preventive measures so that the food crises we experienced in the past do not recur.

This report succeeds in balancing the right to information by strictly defining the nutritional elements that must necessarily appear on labels, with the equally important right to intellectual property for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

I believe manufacturers should indeed be required to immediately notify the use of any new raw material entering the composition of animal feed, in order to ensure transparency and facilitate oversight by the competent authorities.
The inclusion of an emergency procedure enabling the addition of a new hazardous substance to the list of prohibited substances seems entirely indispensable to me.

Similarly, enabling the producer to question the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt regarding an unfair claim further strengthens the system, thereby better protecting the customer while maintaining the integrity of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to fully support this excellent report, as experience shows that regulations on animal feed labeling that prioritize quality, transparency, traceability, and control are the best guarantee for preventing future health crises in Europe.

Ladies and Gentlemen, it is certainly not Europe, nor even its High Representative for Foreign Policy, Javier Solana—former Secretary General of NATO—who can assume the role of mediator between Israel and the Palestinians.
At most, are they expected to fund the reconstruction of the Gaza Strip, as they do today for Kosovo, Lebanon, or Afghanistan?
Americans and Israelis bomb, Europeans fund reconstruction.
Such is the division of tasks among the allies.
Otherwise, it would have to be the vandals who pay.
Egypt is at the heart of peace negotiations concerning a prolonged ceasefire with Hamas.

Yet the challenge ahead is daunting, inasmuch as the new Israeli government, under pressure from the third party—Lieberman, leader of an extreme right-wing faction accepted there democratically—may find its task particularly complicated in these negotiations.
Indeed, Netanyahu, who is presented as the future Prime Minister, has consistently opposed a ceasefire with Hamas.
Another difficulty is that Mahmoud Abbas's Palestinian authority has become in the West Bank a kind of international protectorate whose legitimacy has significantly declined among the population.
The last factor to consider is the ongoing expansion of Israeli settlements, which has never ceased since 1967, making the establishment of a Palestinian state in the West Bank particularly delicate.
Today, the ball is in Israel's court, but do the hardliners on both sides accept this divine truce that both parties demand without taking the necessary steps to make it happen?
Let me add a reflection on France's reintegration into NATO's integrated military structure, which will soon be discussed in the context of the Vatanen report.
This readmission will impose strict obligations on France.
Indeed, we rejoin NATO at a time when the Cold War has been over since 1990.
Sarkozy appears to have forgotten the fall of the Berlin Wall and Russia's return to the ranks of free nations.
Should the logic of blocs be strengthened at a time when multipolarity is increasing and emerging countries are gaining strength, including militarily?
Moreover, France's membership in the integrated structure will oblige it to strengthen its contingent in Afghanistan, even though it already has 3,300 personnel on the ground.
With what budget will it then finance itself, given that its defense budget will fall below 2% of GDP and that more than 30 regiments will be abolished?
Paradoxically, we will increase our budgetary contribution to join NATO while simultaneously reducing our military presence in Africa.
European defense, dear to President Sarkozy, will therefore be a cornerstone of the Atlantic alliance.
One needed only to read the Treaty of Lisbon and its additional protocols to be convinced.
Whether in the realm of foreign policy or common security, the European path is a dead end that will lead only to alignment with the Americans and their allies.
It is this logic of erasure that we reject in the name of sovereignty and national independence, particularly grounded in our autonomous nuclear deterrent capability.
Thank you, Madam President. I congratulate our three rapporteurs for this rather remarkable synthesis they have produced of the current state of our common foreign and security policy.

Mr. Representative, you know better than anyone else that Europe is called upon today to take the place that its economic and demographic strength, together with the richness of its democratic and humanistic values, rightfully assign it on the world stage.

It must be acknowledged that despite the consistent will expressed by more than two-thirds of our European fellow citizens, as you have stated, certain progress has indeed been made over the past ten years; I concede that. Yet it must also be acknowledged that this Europe still does not exist.
If further illustration were needed, his disappearance in the resolution of the new crisis in the Middle East would provide it.
There has always been, and remains today, a need for a European presence in Gaza to actively participate in both supporting the survival of the population and rebuilding the region.
and in the fight against the arms smuggling that had enabled this territory to be transformed into a launch base for rockets of all calibers.
Despite the favorable statements made in Sharm el-Sheikh and Jerusalem, nothing has yet been accomplished in this regard.

I am raising here a question already posed during the Lebanese crisis. When, Mr. High Representative, can we expect the deployment of a European fleet in the Mediterranean, as we had one facing piracy?
We have the means to do so.
Will we ever have the will for it?
Well, Commissioner, Mr. President, my esteemed colleagues, the Parliament's decision to accept the urgency requested by the Council for the
The handling of this file has been unanimous, both at last week's Fisheries Committee and this morning here in plenary session.
We are all indeed aware of the need for the European Union to honor the commitments made in Marrakech last November by the competent international body, the CICTA.
It means concretely implementing the recommendations adopted by this organization in order to halt the persistent problem of overfishing of bluefin tuna in the
in the Eastern Atlantic and in the Mediterranean, and to address the serious dangers arising from them for the future of the species and thus for the fishing industry itself.
Two of our colleagues from the Fisheries Committee, Ms. Fraga, Mr. Romeva, and Mr. Rueda, were present in Marrakech and will speak about it during this debate.
I would like, for my part, to emphasize the importance our committee attaches to this file, which involves the key aspects of the common fisheries policy, the management of
the resource, fleet management, compliance with international, regional, and bilateral agreements, technical measures, and above all, monitoring—I will return to this shortly.
This characteristic file therefore serves as a test for us of the credibility of this common fisheries policy.
For this reason, it was obviously out of the question that the implementation of the new recovery plan take place without consulting the European Parliament.
I therefore welcome the fact that the Commission has ultimately chosen the only legally and politically acceptable path for implementation.
recommendations of the CICTA, namely that of a formal draft regulation based on Article 37 of the Treaty.

The transposition into Community law of commitments undertaken by the Commission on behalf of the European Union within regional fisheries organizations is by no means a trivial exercise, and we insist that it always takes place under the democratic oversight of our institution.
Substantively, I welcome the various measures proposed, which, although binding for our operators, are commensurate with the challenges at hand. And I emphasize that the most significant
The importance of these measures is undoubtedly that which relates to monitoring, as it is true that without monitoring, no restoration plan can be effective.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, I thank you for this proposal. I invite you to show the same
The determination in implementing the plan concretely, once it has been endorsed by the Council, which you have demonstrated in reaching a satisfactory agreement at the level of the CICTA.
Thank you, Madam President. Commissioner, I regret that the Council and the Commission have not yet taken concrete steps to define territorial cohesion.
The French presidency is being tried there, and I would like to commend this initiative.
Our Parliament continues to insist that this objective be applicable from the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon and that its parameters be clarified as soon as possible.

I wish, like the excellent report by Mr. Van Nistelrooy, that territorial cohesion become the legal basis ensuring the balanced development of all territories of the Union and enabling the optimal utilization of the specific characteristics of each European region.
It is essential to better coordinate community policies in order to maximize the impact of on-the-ground interventions.
Territorial cohesion is not intended to target exclusively regions suffering from permanent disadvantages.
It must nevertheless focus on the polycentric development of the European Union as a whole, taking into account the specific characteristics of each territory, while preserving their diversity.
This new concept appears relevant for taking into account the ultra-peripheral regions, in order to ensure their sustainable and balanced growth.

In this regard, I thank the rapporteur for having included in his compromise amendments my requests concerning the specific challenges faced by the outermost regions in terms of accessibility and competitiveness—essential elements for territorial cohesion.
Upon reading the Commission's recent communication entitled "The URP: An Asset for Europe," it appears to me that it intends to apply all available resources to the ultra-periphery.
recommendations regarding the improvement of governance in cohesion matters, thus making the RUPs the first, somewhat anticipatory, illustration of the implementation of territorial cohesion.

I wish that the Overseas General Assembly, which will soon begin in France, proceed in the same manner and extensively incorporate the major territorial impact of European policies in favor of regions, whose added value is undeniable.

One enjoys reading the conclusions of the presidency of the last Council.
It resembles the victory statement issued by every army in retreat.

They display such absurdity even in their repeated assertions that, once the crisis ends, the EU will build a stronger economy for the future, or in their reaffirmation of a Lisbon Strategy in the midst of total collapse.
Thus, as they confine themselves within their glass tower, eurocrats, europhiles, and all manner of europhiles ultimately lose their grasp on reality.
Everyone knows well that the eurozone was the first to enter recession, having experienced for eight years the world's lowest interest rate.
but it is also the one facing the greatest difficulties in terms of industrial competitiveness and employment conditions.
Above all, it is she who, by the general disarmament of the natural defenses stripped from nations, is the least armed to respond.

Only states will possess the legitimate tools necessary for any large-scale action, and to achieve this they will need to challenge the constraints imposed by the EU and the euro.

For the French, at any rate, it is becoming increasingly clear that there can be no salvation without France, no France without French policy, and no French policy without national and popular sovereignty. Thank you, Mr. President.

Thank you, Madam President. I believe the question of distributing food supplies to the most disadvantaged is indeed timely; however, I think, at heart, the Commission's proposal does not address the actual issue.
The issue of co-financing troubles me, and I find myself thinking that it is rather the liberalism underlying the evaluation of the CAP in recent years that should be called into question.
Food riots and abrupt fluctuations in food prices are permanent indicators of the limits of agricultural market liberalization.
This liberalization has always occurred at the expense of vulnerable populations, vulnerable producers, and fragile territories.

I therefore advocate that politics reclaim its rightful role, regain control, both at the European and global levels, and ensure that the strategic domain of agriculture and human food, both in quantitative and qualitative terms, is not subjected solely to the rigid laws of the market.
Certainly, there is urgency, and the Sikorsky report responds to this urgency, which I welcome; however, I wish that within the framework of the reflection now opening on the
Regarding the future of the CAP, it should be recalled that the primary objective is firmly to combat hunger and poverty structurally, as we experience them in all Member States and worldwide.

Rural areas are capable of meeting food needs through sufficient and high-quality production. However, they will need the necessary financial and human resources to fulfill this significant responsibility.
Thank you, Mr. President. During this period of economic recession, it was important that the European Union continue to demonstrate its solidarity with developing countries.
Let us recall that in 2007, the European community and its member states jointly contributed 60% of total development aid, making the European Union the world's leading donor.
European development funds enable the fight against poverty while promoting economic development and democracy.
I therefore welcome the allocation of a budget of 23 billion euros for ACP countries for the 2008–2013 period under the 10th EDF, which amounts to almost double the sum provided for the 9th EDF.
The audit, in its report, concludes that the accounts of the 7th, 8th, and 9th FED are generally accurate, lawful, and regular.
One can only welcome the record levels of contract implementation and payment disbursement for the FED.
However, I also observe that further progress must be made to strengthen the control and monitoring system, as a significant level of error still affects certain transactions.
Likewise, there is too frequently an excessive risk regarding financial support, and in my view, these risks should be better assessed.
On this matter, I share the view that budgetary support should only be granted if the recipient country has the capacity to manage the funds in a transparent, responsible, and efficient manner.

Among the priorities on which we must focus in the future, I would like to highlight the integration of the EDF into the community's general budget, as this would strengthen the effectiveness and transparency of development assistance.

To conclude, Mr. President, I would also like to thank the rapporteur for his excellent report, the representatives of the Court, as well as every individual from the Commission who has carried out outstanding work on the ground. I naturally regret the absence of the Council, and in particular that of the Czech Presidency.
Madam President, the Parliament and its rapporteur were compelled to reject a poor compromise. They were right to do so.
Our delegation has remained faithful to the position widely adopted during the plenary session, which called for the eventual elimination of all exceptions to the legal working week.
But the truth is that Parliament was the only one wanting to move things forward.
The representatives of the European people had ruled well beyond partisan divisions to put an end to this archaism known as the opt-out.
We were the only ones to propose a genuine compromise text, which the Council of Ministers superbly ignored, just as the Commission did.
The Council and the Commission have aligned themselves with those who fiercely oppose any progress in workers' rights in Europe.
Mr. President of the Council, it is clear that you have achieved your objectives.
The Court of Justice requires you to regard all custody time as working time. You have therefore lost nothing in this regard.
The opt-out you want still exists in practice; thus, the status quo has prevailed.
More than ever, the deputies elected in June will have to strive to achieve upward harmonization of social standards.

Mr. President, this is certainly not on behalf of the Libyan Commission, but rather most likely on behalf of my group. I would like to take this brief moment to express my exceptionally positive assessment of Mrs. Paganoff's report.
Congratulations, Madam, on your report.

I believe that, in writing this report, you have developed an extraordinarily detailed specification of elements that should rightfully be included in the Stockholm Programme, Mr. Commissioner, which I know the Commission is actively preparing.

Allow me to add, in addition to everything that has been said regarding the importance of judicial training and evaluation, two fundamental elements concerning mutual trust, which lie at the foundation of what could become mutual recognition in the future.
1. The independence of the judiciary.
At present, there are several countries within the Union where the independence of the judiciary from political power or from other powers has not yet been established.
It is a scandal, and this scandal must end.
2. Procedural safeguards.

As long as we are not assured that in a certain number of countries, individuals suspected or accused of committing certain types of offences enjoy procedural safeguards similar to those in place in other countries, it will be difficult to achieve broad acceptance of the principle of mutual recognition.
It is a fundamental aspect, and I wanted to introduce it into this discussion.
Congratulations, Ms. Pagano.
Madam President, we cannot remain silent about the situation in Madagascar. And we will not be alone in condemning this situation.
The African Union, the Southern African Development Community, the International Organization of La Francophonie, the Inter-Parliamentary Union, the European Union, the Commission speaking,

The United States, a large number of countries including my own and Norway, speaking of countries from the European continent, have condemned the coup d'état, because one must call a spade a spade, which took place in Madagascar.
Therefore, we cannot remain silent, and we call for a return to constitutional legality.
We never demand a return to the original class, with, if necessary, an arbitration by the Malagasy people through a consultation in the form of a presidential election or a referendum.
It is up to Madagascar's assemblies and political leaders to determine the most effective form of consultation.

Therefore, what we are requesting through this joint resolution is to add our voice to that of the international community in clearly signaling to those who have seized power in a wholly undemocratic manner,

democratic manner and, in the form of a coup d'état however well-disguised it may be, to restore Madagascar's constitutional legality, which is one of the guarantees for the future development of this great island in the Indian Ocean.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, of course, I would like to thank Commissioner Dimas for his presence and his answers, and for the attention he continues to give to all these disasters.
We worked closely together when President Barroso asked me to prepare a report, which he subsequently cited, on the establishment of a European civil protection force.
I presented this report on May 9, 2006.
And when I reread it and see all the disasters that have occurred, I realize it remains ever relevant.
Madame Zanlella, of course, there is more than just Greece.
All countries are affected. And I would add that there are not only natural disasters, but also human-made disasters caused by people.
I think of maritime disasters, the RICAS and countless others, of industrial accidents such as Chernobyl.
There are other natural disasters besides fires.
I think of the tsunami,
We may one day fear a tsunami in the Mediterranean, as occurred at the beginning of the 20th century, which completely destroyed the city of Messina.
Frankly, Commissioner, my dear colleagues, the tools we are deploying are not up to the scale of these disasters, which will multiply under the dual impact of climate change and transportation.
And that is why I believe we must aim higher.
I thank the Commission, Commissioner Dimas, all their teams, and the Directorate-General for operating the Civil Protection mechanism.
I believe we need to go further.
with the member countries that wish to do so. One can envision enhanced cooperation
If we cannot muster 27 people to form this civil protection unit, let us begin with 12 or 15.
And then you will see that it will concern everyone.
When I list the disasters, I can say that everyone is affected. The floods that struck Germany in 2002, even major pandemics, and also terrorism.
Here you are, Commissioner, the reason why I would like the Commission to take the initiative to go further, and to propose to the Member States that they go further as well.
And our Parliament, the Committee on the Environment, and the Committee on Defense and Security will support you in these proactive initiatives.

Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament, once again this summer, the Mediterranean forest has been devastated by flames in Marseille, in southern Corsica, as well as in Italy, Spain, and on a larger scale in Greece.
Over 400,000 hectares of forests and fields went up in flames, triggering substantial material losses, irreversible damage to biodiversity, and dramatic human casualties.
disturbed by this repeated annual assessment and aware of the multiplying effect of climate change on wildfires,
I call for a European awareness-raising effort to improve the coordination of actions and the pooling of resources for fighting wildfires.
I wish to see the realization of a large-scale project for euro-Mediterranean cooperation in combating forest fires within the framework of the Union for the Mediterranean.
I welcome existing initiatives, in particular the European Forest Fire Information System, a pilot project,
I nonetheless call for a reform of the European Union Solidarity Fund so that it becomes an effective tool for responding to crises and a reinforcement of European strength.
protection and the EuroAid mechanism, conceived by Michel Barnier as early as 2006, designed to operate across the territory of the 27 member countries as well as abroad.

I believe one of the keys to addressing the problem of forest fires lies in the exchange of knowledge and best practices in prevention. I would like to highlight the pioneering role played by the forest firefighter personnel in my own department.
And I can obviously make available to Europe this example, which may be useful. Thank you.
Good morning, Madam President, good morning, Madam Commissioner. Well, I'll be brief.

The conjunctural issue: how to get out of this crisis?
And all measures that simultaneously assist farmers and involve intervention are necessary and absolutely necessary,
they must be supported, and even considered more significant,

There is no other alternative.
It is here that the debate will have to take place after this crisis.
And I believe that on this issue, we must respect all opinions and all options. And I fear that we moved too quickly at the time of the health check to resolve a matter such as quotas.

And so far, I say this to all those who have contributed: this policy has simultaneously maintained dairy production in Europe, ensured an extremely developed dairy industry, and at the same time provided consumers with prices that have so far been entirely affordable. Mr. President, just a few days after the positive referendum result in Ireland, I truly feel that we can say today that we are more European than we were before.
I truly, fully, and serenely rejoice in it.
Unfortunately, we remain essentially dependent on the decision of the Constitutional Court, as well as on the goodwill of the Czech President.
We must send a clear signal of accountability to those who hold in their hands the future of this pivotal treaty for the Union, for our institutions, and for the 500 million Europeans we represent.
We can no longer be hostages to a few individuals whose sole aim is to undermine the general interest of Europe.

While it is legitimate for some to wish to go no further in European integration, and while it is legitimate to express such reservations, it is equally legitimate to allow those who wish it to move forward.
In general, the treaty ratification processes within Europe often take on the character of a saga or a Pyrrhic victory.
Are we doomed to constantly have to exert pressure to ensure the implementation of a treaty and its institutional reforms?
We must draw lessons from this chaotic ratification process, as well as, of course, from this commitment to negotiation aimed at organizing a second ratification.

referendum in Ireland, as if it were normal to make a people vote again after they had expressed their sovereign will, and as if it were normal for consent to a treaty could be used as a bargaining chip.
It is a matter of the coherence and institutional balance of the European venture.
Ratifying a treaty is a minimal requirement of loyalty and cohesion once one has chosen for one's country to join the European Union.
We wanted to send a strong signal to citizens by ratifying the treaty for the June 2009 European elections.
What do you think today's citizen thinks of the Treaty of Lisbon, and beyond our internal coherence and legitimacy?
Thank you, Mr. President. My intervention concerns the deterioration of working conditions in Europe.
Today, nearly half of the workers worldwide earn less than two dollars a day.
12.3 million people are still subjected to slavery, and more than 200 million children are forced to work.
In Europe, the number of working poor increases day by day, and the International Labour Organization records 160,000 deaths annually due to lack of prevention.
In this context, I would like to emphasize our responsibility toward all those who are unemployed and deprived of social rights, who work under degrading conditions.
of these millions of workers who suffer mentally and physically, sometimes driven to suicide, or victims of cancers or chronic illnesses due to their working conditions.
It is time to move beyond the religion of working more, the pursuit of profit, and short-term competition.
The right to decent work and the strengthening of the right to work must be at the top of the Union's priorities over the next ten years.
Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, a challenge for humanity, a challenge for future generations.

To rise to the challenge, Copenhagen must enable a comprehensive, single agreement, as our Committee on the Environment Chair, Jolaï Nenoui, just emphasized. A coalition encompassing all countries—industrialized nations and emerging economies—is essential.

Today, through our debate, as well as the adoption of a resolution by our Parliament, we once again call on the European Union to maintain its leading role in this essential policy of combating climate change.
Let us recall that we must speak with one voice in order to maintain our credibility.
Yes, we must reach an agreement in Copenhagen to limit the increase in global average temperature to a level that does not exceed 2 degrees above pre-industrial levels.
Yes, we must in Copenhagen sign an agreement aimed at achieving collective greenhouse gas emission reductions of 30% by 2020 compared to 1997.
But that will not be enough.
It is not only necessary for developed countries to significantly reduce their emissions, but also essential for developing countries to contribute to achieving these goals.

Industrialized countries must therefore provide sufficient, durable, and predictable financial and technical support to developing countries to encourage them to commit to reducing their greenhouse gas emissions.
Copenhagen must therefore enable developing countries to transition toward low-carbon models.
Therefore, allow me to address two avenues that appear necessary for our future.
First, the establishment at the global level of climate change mitigation financing based on taxing financial transactions.
Subsequently, it seems necessary to implement a border tax adjustment for Europe that will pre-charge products manufactured without the slightest regard for environmental protection.

a contractualization and transfer to the Global South of the carbon tariff collected at Europe's borders, which would be used to finance investments in the infrastructure necessary to combat global warming

Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to speak along the same lines as my intervention yesterday regarding the preparation for the Copenhagen summit, and further in light of yesterday's Ecofin meeting, which was not successful.
I have addressed to you the responsibility we bear as industrialized nations to ensure that developing countries can keep pace with our efforts to combat climate change.
In Copenhagen, we cannot pretend that we all have the same capabilities. Otherwise, it would mean that we all start from the same point and ultimately achieve nothing.
Therefore, to persuade our development partners to join us, we must obviously address the issue of the aid they must receive, and not wait for the outcome of the Copenhagen summit.
At the next European Council, the 27 must absolutely agree on how the European Union envisions assistance to developing countries within the significant context of climate change.
Yesterday, therefore, as I was saying, the ministerial adviser Coffin failed to find a common position on this issue.
Yet, we know that this question is absolutely central.

The European Commission had already made proposals. I absolutely wish for us to take the initiative in guiding our partners toward the global solidarity necessary to overcome this challenge of climate change.
The efforts of the Swedish presidency to achieve an ambitious agreement in Copenhagen, despite the strong current of pessimism blowing through these future negotiations.
In my view, it is not realistic to admit at this stage that the Copenhagen summit is already dead or that it will fail to produce a binding agreement acceptable to all parties.
Let us not yield too readily to pessimism.
I advocate that, at this stage, we should not lower our ambitions, either in terms of goals or deadlines.
We must continue to believe in a comprehensive, ambitious, and binding global political agreement, paving the way for the earliest possible conclusion of a legal treaty.
It is truly too early to discuss the deadline for COP16 in December 2010.
We must now translate our rhetoric into genuine political will.
We must clarify our commitment in financial terms and in terms of assistance to developing countries, particularly regarding technology transfers.
At this stage, a total and collective commitment from the European Union is indispensable.

I further note on paper the recent but exemplary initiative taken by countries such as Brazil, the Republic of Korea, Indonesia, Norway, among others, which have quantified their ambitions to reduce polluting emissions.

While one may understand the flexibility demanded by the United States, which has only just begun to address the climate issue, we must nevertheless secure concrete, binding, and ambitious commitments from other major polluters—such as the United States and China—across short-, medium-, and long-term horizons.
Failing that, we are headed toward climatic, political, and moral disaster.
Madam President, we are all aware, and we have all stated, the importance of the Copenhagen negotiations; but while we all hope for an agreement, we cannot accept an agreement at any price.
If the conditions for an ambitious agreement are not met, I clearly wish for the European Union to be able to say no and refuse to sign a substandard agreement.
The European populations we represent here are awaiting an agreement, an agreement that would enable us to effectively combat the full range of climate disruptions we observe around us every day.
They will not be satisfied with mere announcements, non-specific statements of intent, or non-binding objectives that can be conveniently disregarded once the media spotlight has faded.

In Copenhagen, states will have to commit. The European Union, the only continent to have seen its CO2 emissions decrease since 1990 and the only continent to set precise, binding, and ambitious future commitments, cannot bear the burden of the effort alone.

This is why, if other industrialized and emerging countries are unwilling to assume their share of the burden, we must fully address the consequences by imposing at our borders a tax that safeguards our industry against competition from those who choose not to participate in a global agreement.
We, Europeans, are increasingly demanding more from our industrialists every day.
For proof, I cite the Climate Package that was adopted during the French presidency.
I cite as evidence the national initiatives, like the carbon tax, praised a moment ago by Mr. Parniaud and which I welcome, that promote the establishment of an environmental tax system.

A sector such as transportation, which is among the major emitters of CO2, contributes increasingly to the fight against climate change, even though certain modes of transport, such as aviation, emit fewer greenhouse gases.
Indeed, this is a sector that has been severely affected by the repercussions of the crisis.

If, as I hope, maritime and air transport are included in the negotiations in Copenhagen, then let us ensure that what is required of European industries is also required of those in other industrialized countries.
In Copenhagen, world public opinion will be focused on its ruling class.
Madam President, my esteemed colleagues, the Copenhagen summit is a historic opportunity to change the course of the planet.
We must reach decisions even more binding than those adopted in Kyoto.

We too must call on our leaders to establish an international organization such as the WTO, entrusted with regulating issues related to climate change and the environment, and to create a fundamentalism.
to climate change, in addition to public development aid, to establish a maritime and aviation carbon tax, as we should likewise do for financial transactions.
We clearly see the pressing need for global regulation of the environment, just as for financial markets and the Internet.
We have the opportunity to begin this march of history with the environment.
Let us do it, in keeping with our highest human aspirations, otherwise we shall appear as mere officials, disconnected from the realities of life. Thank you.

Thank you, Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, my dear colleagues. With less than two weeks until the Copenhagen conference, concerns are growing regarding the success of the climate change negotiations.
Some others, already, claim that it will be a failure.
Major global powers still hesitate to demonstrate the full ambition that the climate emergency demands.

By adopting tomorrow the resolution proposal on the European Union's strategy ahead of the Copenhagen conference, we, European parliamentarians, must demonstrate that our determination is absolute, and that the conference must lead to an agreement that is not only precise, but above all binding.
Moreover, the binding nature of this agreement, once established, must not be subject to challenge or dispute.

We cannot indefinitely postpone decision-making until tomorrow, otherwise it will be too late. Hence the necessary creation of a global environmental organization, which today appears not only essential but also urgent, as it will be established under the auspices of the United Nations to monitor the implementation of commitments made in Copenhagen.
From Copenhagen onwards, we must be able to expect greater pressure from this world, along with greater clarity and responsibility.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to express my support for the joint initiative by the British Labour government and the French government to return illegal Afghan nationals to their home country.

Indeed, contrary to what some people have claimed, these individuals had already had their application for political asylum rejected. This bilateral initiative must now be extended at the European level.
Migration pressure is a challenge we must collectively rise to meet. This is precisely the purpose of the migration and asylum pact, adopted a few months ago under French presidency.
We must now go further by pooling our resources and organizing shared return flights funded by Frontex.

The decision by the 27 to request the Commission undertake a study on this matter is a first step. Mr. Commissioner, I urge the Commission to support this proposal, as combating illegal immigration is a public expectation, particularly among regular migrant populations. Thank you.
Mr. President, I would like to return to a recent event, the Swiss referendum on minarets.
Certainly, this vote took place in a state not belonging to the European Union, but it is clear that it touches upon an issue that is not foreign to us.
I would like to return to this event, as I also see it as an opportunity to discuss secularism, and we never speak enough about secularism within these walls.
It is necessary and urgent that the principle of secularism govern the organization of our society. And I would like to say here some simple things.
First of all, the opposite principle of secularism is not religiosity, nor even spirituality.
The principle contrary to secularism is religious communitarianism, which establishes rules and laws superior to civil laws. Communitarianism that confines individuals within sub-societal groups.
Our union, by virtue of its history, is multicultural and multilingual.
It will become increasingly so, and only secularism can enable individuals...
The César programme, which aims to modernize air traffic management in Europe, is a major project.
How will the Commission prepare the next phase of Caesar's redeployment, and in particular the funding of this essential program for both the environment and our scientific and technical standing?
Will the Commission follow the recommendations of the 2007 report, which stated that a high-level qualified individual should be responsible for the political oversight of this program?
Indeed, it will require overcoming numerous legal, psychological, and technical obstacles, unifying national airspace and enabling air traffic controllers from different countries to work together.
If Europe is incapable of unifying its skies, how could it possibly build a strong political entity?
Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Minister, dear colleagues. The importance of the issue of climate change no longer needs to be proven.

These short-, medium-, or long-term impacts are undeniable, even though today's results from the GESC published in a certain national journal are making headlines and undoubtedly strengthen the positions of skeptics.
The Copenhagen summit appeared to the world as a unique opportunity for global leaders to bear witness, take action, and address the effects of the process that is climate change.

The European Union was at the forefront of this podium, not only because of the bold legislation we have in place regarding the limitation of our activities' impact on the climate, but also because Europe represents a union of countries that can truly make a difference.
Certainly, the outcome of the Copenhagen conference can only leave a taste of dissatisfaction, but I refuse to speak of failure.
The international community has reached consensus on limiting the increase in global temperature to 2°C, even among the most hesitant countries, such as China.
Brazil has just enacted a law aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions by nearly 39% by 2020, in line with its commitments.
Many others have also demonstrated significant ambitions regarding greenhouse gas emission reduction targets,
I am thinking of Japan and Norway.
Moreover, it is also necessary to emphasize this very positive aspect: the consensus achieved regarding the urgency of establishing a mechanism to reduce emissions resulting from deforestation.
A specific and unanimous text has been established regarding the implementation of the Direct Plus mechanism to reduce deforestation and thereby enable natural carbon dioxide storage.
Far from a binding agreement, but clearly in line with a political understanding, the outcome of Copenhagen must lead us to reflect on the reasons for such disappointment.
I attended the Copenhagen conference, like many of my colleagues, with the intention of participating, contributing, and witnessing a negotiating Europe, a leading Europe in action.
My disappointment was great, as Europe clearly did not receive the place it deserved in the negotiations, and all the more so when it comes to the European Parliament.
To assert itself, Europe must demonstrate greater strength on this issue.
The meeting of our ministers on the environment in Seville did not yield results. Our officials have appeared uncertain and hesitant, and we remain divided on our objectives.
I hope that Ms. Edegarde, drawing on her proposals and commitments made before the Environment Committee, will succeed in raising the ambitions of the European Union to the required level.

Thank you. I now give the floor to Mr. Bouddhiste. Dear colleagues, in order to avoid caricatures—some of which we have already heard this morning—I would like to recall a few objective facts regarding Tunisia, a partner country of the European Union.
Social conditions have improved significantly, with a spectacular rise in average income per capita, and now the vast majority of Tunisians meet the criteria defining membership in the middle class.

Over 90% of Tunisians have social coverage, and more than a quarter of Tunisia's budget is allocated to education, placing the country among the first to achieve the Millennium Development Goals for primary education.
Finally, Mr. Michel reminded us just now that women's rights are recognized and guaranteed.
At university, female students outnumber male students. 40% of professors are women. One quarter of parliamentarians, local officials, or journalists are women.
Many countries could envy such results.

Thank you, Mr. President. Less than 15% of the original bluefin tuna stock remains today.
Faced with such figures, the solution appears obvious.
That said, we must not forget the jobs affected by the decision before us today.
It should be recalled that CITES is not intended to ban fishing, but only the international trade of this fish, 80% of which is exported to Japan.

By safeguarding tuna, we certainly protect a species dangerously overfished, but we also promote a more balanced and sustainable fishing activity, fishing intended for our domestic market and capable of generating employment.

I am in favor of the idea of listing the red drum in Appendix 1 of CITES. And although I believe this decision will be positive, the support of the Commission to enable a thorough restructuring of the fishing sector will be essential.
My question therefore concerns the terms of this support.
France requests an 18-month postponement, accompanied by financial measures for mariners and shipowners affected by a trade ban.
What is the Commission's vision on this matter?
Moreover, my concern, like that of many of my colleagues, is fairness.
How can we ensure that vessels flying the flags of countries such as Tunisia, Libya, or others will enforce the international trade ban with a level of rigor comparable to our own?
What will the new measures for control and sanctions be?

Our policy on the sustainability of economic activities must be concrete, and at times even unpopular in certain sectors, but I hope the Commission and the Council do not lose sight of the necessary adjustments required for implementing these measures.

Thank you, Mr. President. Commissioner, my esteemed colleagues, as the rapporteur has reported, this proposal for a directive indeed follows upon a vote by Parliament, a point you have also recalled, Commissioner.
Yet, my group remains highly critical of this proposal because it risks backfiring against those we aim to support.
By seeking to reduce the burdens on businesses, we risk depriving them of the conditions of transparency and trust essential to their management and the vitality of their operations.
First of all, I believe it is essential to be clear about the thresholds and about what is referred to as micro-entities.
These are SMEs with more than 10 employees and more than €1 million in turnover,
Regardless of the member state, this represents a very large majority of companies.

Certainly, they may be local and of little presence on the community market, but this does not alter the fact that the need to establish accounting rules will not be abolished for them, and that these
accounting rules, they will have to confront them whenever they wish to deal with a banking institution, with partners, or sometimes negotiate with fiscal, economic, and social institutions.
At that point, accounting rules will be imposed upon them, and they may end up paying more than they would for regular accounting in order to meet criteria they may not necessarily master.
I do not see any progress in imposing accounting obligations outside of any common framework within the European Union.
This is not merely a matter of competition. It is fundamentally a question of economic integration and community law, as well as equal treatment for all enterprises throughout the European Union.
That said, obviously, simplifying accounting obligations for SMEs is a necessity.
Three other initiatives also point in this direction, whether it be the proposal we are discussing today, or the international framework IFR.
whether it be the revision of the fourth and seventh accounting directives, for which the European Commission should soon be presenting us with proposals.
These proposals were promised to us for 2010; it seems they have been extended, but I do not believe this extension of the deadline is a sufficient reason for this...
to rush and do half a job. For these three proposals, although they share the same objective, may not have the same structure. And it seems to me, Mr. Commissioner,

and clearer for businesses, providing them with a single, comprehensive solution that will enable them to balance the shared goal of simplifying obligations with the practical realities faced by small businesses that rely on such support.
The current issue is that the European Commission is delaying its proposal for a revision of these directives, and this delay is detrimental to our deliberations.

I therefore call on the European Commission to accelerate the dialogue regarding the issue of exemption, which is currently dividing both European institutions and economic circles, and to carry out an impact assessment.

Mr. President, Commissioner, and dear colleagues, As my colleague Elisabeth Morin-Chartier and other colleagues have indicated, on February 27 and 28, the violent storm Cynthia struck several regions of France, resulting in 53 fatalities and extensive material damage.
The storm was of exceptional intensity. It caused devastating floods along the French coastline.
After Madeira, Europe is once again affected.
Brittany, the region of my colleague and friend Alain Cadec, has been severely affected
Three French departments have thus been declared to be in a state of natural disaster: Brittany, Poitou-Charentes, and the Loire regions.
In the face of these disasters, the European Union must demonstrate its responsiveness and express its solidarity.
It is not only through words and a resolution, but above all through financial assistance, that we will be able to support the victims of these disasters.
