I therefore invite, and my colleague Christophe Béchus joins me in this request, the European Commission to swiftly release funds from the Union's Solidarity Fund to assist the affected regions in coping with the damages.
This catastrophe underscores the relevance of Michel Barnier's proposal to establish a European civilian intervention force.

Europeans must act together, as disasters are increasing in number and affecting our European territories, particularly our most isolated and vulnerable regions, such as the islands in the Indian Ocean and the Caribbean.

Madam President, I too would like to congratulate our rapporteur, Jörg Leichtfried, on his text and his spirit of cooperation on this file. I particularly support his proposals regarding the funding of aviation security, since we are discussing aviation security, not aviation safety.
Our Commission has been advocating for many years for the principle that stricter security measures must be borne by the Member States. I fully support our rapporteur on this issue.
Indeed, security concerns not only travelers but all citizens. It is the responsibility of states to take measures to combat terrorism.
It is therefore unjustified that air passengers alone should bear the cost of security measures at airports and on board aircraft.
And this is all the more so because, in modes of transport such as rail and maritime, states partially or fully fund security measures, depending on the case.
And thus, I believe we must remain firm before the Council and defend, to the very end, amendment 32 of the report adopted by our transport committee.
Then I had another point to briefly address

While I support the progress made by this text in terms of non-discrimination and transparency, I do not believe the objective of this directive is to establish a single, uniform European system for safety fees.
While requiring compliance with common rules, Member States must be allowed to retain their current system for collecting royalties.

And thus, in countries such as Spain, Italy, or France, royalties are approved by Parliament. In such cases, the authority responsible for reviewing laws must serve as the independent supervisory body.
This is the purpose of Amendment 40, which I hope you will all support when we vote on it.
Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. The report I am presenting to Parliament seeks to explain that, in the fight against climate change, agriculture has a role to play and a place to occupy.
This week, nature reminds us that volcanoes, in particular, can have a significant impact on human activities and on climate.
But this nonetheless requires that men, and Europe in particular, take their responsibilities in combating what is known as global warming.
My report seeks to demonstrate that agriculture can simultaneously continue to reduce its carbon emissions, decrease its consumption of fossil energy, and, above all, adopt a different approach.
On the one hand, it has actively contributed to carbon fixation, thereby globally reducing carbon emissions and increasing the amount of carbon fixed and sequestered.
This report attempts to show that in agriculture, we should move away from a, how shall I put it, compartmentalized approach—where every time we face a problem, we simply open a new directive—
on the soil, on water, on pesticides, who knows what else, and to attempt to approach the agricultural issue with the ambition of addressing it in a comprehensive, systemic manner.

And in this approach, we must be able to identify pathways to ensure that agriculture, as I mentioned earlier, not only reduces its energy consumption but also actively contributes to carbon sequestration.
It is particularly the role related to photosynthesis and biomass, and above all, what is most important to me, the connection to soil—the capacity of European soils to sequester organic carbon.

And I believe that the path I am opening in this report, and which I hope will be followed during the vote to be held in plenary in Brussels, must enable European agriculture to enter a phase of sustainability, and ensure a

ecological transition for this agriculture, placing it ahead of many other continents or agricultural systems, so that it can be economically efficient, ecologically sound, and, I believe, also socially effective from that point onward.

Thus, this report addresses a specific issue—the fight against climate change and agriculture—but it also aspires, I hope, to open up other perspectives, particularly to contribute to the debate on the future of agricultural policy after 2013.
And I address myself to the Commission, I will address, and I have often done so, the agricultural committees, and I will also address the various ministers whenever the opportunity arises,
I think we need to make a turn.
This 2013 CAP must be ambitious.
It must be based on objectives that enable citizens to understand why we need a common agricultural policy.
We need a common agricultural policy because Europe's agriculture must ensure food security, while also committing to a sustainable path.
This is at least what I propose in this report, and this is what I hope the Parliament, by a large majority, will follow.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would first like to emphasize that the precautionary principle implemented by most European governments—closing their airspace in a targeted and temporary manner—was a wise and prudent decision.

The safety of our fellow citizens must take precedence over all other considerations. Under these circumstances, the stance of certain airlines demanding the immediate and full reopening of airspace, even for just one or two test flights, appears at the very least indecent.

Then I believe it was Mr. Hénin earlier who spoke about the complementarity of transport modes, particularly with regard to trains. I shall take this opportunity to express how much I regret the air transport paralysis...
has been exacerbated in certain countries such as France by the disruption of railway transport due to strikes under these irresponsible and incomprehensible circumstances.
Returning to our subject, I wish to welcome the Commission's decision to authorize the granting of public aid to airline companies affected by the ongoing disruptions,
It is a sensible decision in a context already marked by crisis, but these measures must be of an exceptional nature.
And in this regard, I wish that among the criteria selected for ROC 3 is the exemplary conduct demonstrated by airlines in compensating customers who have suffered flight cancellations.
Indeed, it is unacceptable for certain airlines to abuse the force majeure clause in order to evade their obligation to compensate passengers under Regulation 261/2004.
Travelers are victims of the current situation, and if no alternative solution is offered to them, they must not additionally bear the financial burden.
Furthermore, travel agencies are exempt from the obligation to refund unused flights to travelers.
And this is not normal either.

Companies such as travel agencies have insurance coverage that protects them in exceptional circumstances, such as the one we have been experiencing over the past few days, and it is therefore essential to ensure that travelers are properly compensated for flights that have been canceled.
Thank you, Mr. President. Chaos, disorder, paralysis, cacophony, catastrophe, disaster—there is no shortage of words in the press to describe the closure of European airspace and its consequences.
I will not dwell on what has already been said, particularly regarding the immense, abyssal—one might even say—financial impact of this crisis, direct or indirect.

Although I welcome the possibility of considering exceptional public aid for the aviation sector, already widely approved since September 11, 2001, I remain nonetheless perplexed by the European handling of these events.

Knowing that 750,000 European passengers have been affected, a significant number of whom remain stranded across the globe. Knowing that economic losses grow exponentially with time. How can one explain that it took not longer...
not one, two, three, or four, but five days, so that European transport ministers could meet via teleconference to coordinate their actions and decide on the creation of differentiated traffic zones.
Two, no one disputes the necessity of applying the precautionary principle with absolute priority. But today, as traffic is gradually being reinstated in certain areas

Secure, with unchanged weather conditions and the volcano still active, one is justified in questioning what additional safety assurances for passengers we could not have provided earlier or more swiftly.

Three. An evolving action model has been developed. Given that conditions may change hourly depending on weather and volcanic activity, who will continue to conduct updated assessments of corridor safety?
Should these test flights be carried out by civilian aviation and airlines?

Finally, if it persists, worsens, or recurs— which is likely—the management model implemented will need to provide for greater coordination among member states and special operational procedures based on real-time data, as well as improved

coordination of other transport means under exceptional circumstances, but let us also take into account the necessary coordination of assistance for the tens of thousands of stranded passengers, who likewise have the right to information and support.
Or, to date, only isolated and national initiatives have been taken.

Thank you, Madam President. I would like, in turn, after other speakers, to express my gratitude to our colleague Mendès Divigo, who has carried out remarkable work under sometimes passionate and always fascinating circumstances.

Debates within the Committee on Conventional Arms lasted a long time. They were commensurate with the importance of the issue and matched the intensity and interest undoubtedly demonstrated by the members.

European parliamentarians on the major issue that concerns them above all else in a perfectly legitimate manner, the composition of our assembly and the procedures for appointing members of our assembly.

Once again, the rapporteur has succeeded in bringing together sometimes differing opinions and contributions, and synthesizing, I would say, in an extremely balanced text, the various views and perspectives.
I wanted to thank him for it.

I believe the recommendations contained in these two reports—both the one concerning the convening of the IGC and the one regarding transitional measures for the composition of our Assembly—are marked by realism, pragmatism, and effectiveness.
I believe this is, overall, what citizens also expect from Europe on other issues.
Mr. President, Mr. Dali, Commissioner, this draft report, as presented just now by Mr. Péterlé, strongly reaffirms the guiding principles
Director of Communication at the European Commission, and also drawing on the European Parliament resolution of 10 April 2008 on the fight against cancer in our European Union.

Allow me to support the objectives of the European partnership envisioned by the European Commission to more effectively combat cancer, including the importance of prevention and early detection, the development of a new model for cancer prevention, and especially the reduction of inequalities among Member States.
I share the concerns and apprehensions expressed in the European Commission's communication and the draft report.

I would like to commend Mr. Péterlé, the rapporteur, for the work he has accomplished in drafting this report, as well as the proposed compromise solutions intended to incorporate the various amendments.

As rapporteur fictif for the Socialist and Democratic Group, I wished, among other things, to highlight the following issues. First, the increase in annual cancer-related deaths caused by exposure to carcinogenic agents in the workplace, as well as the importance of improved access to information.

containing carcinogenic chemicals, and to strengthen monitoring of the presence of chemical substances within the European Union. Finally, the development of guidelines for a common definition of disability that could include individuals suffering from chronic illnesses or cancer.
Here are the questions we have raised while supporting this draft report.
Yes, thank you, Madam President. The oil well leak in the Gulf of Mexico is a true ecological and economic catastrophe.
It appears that irregularities may have been detected in the issuance by the U.S. Department of the Interior of forest permits.
This would constitute an unforgivable lapse, the consequences of which are incalculable and affect not only the United States of America.
Given that hundreds of offshore drilling permits are granted by states around the world, if these permits were issued with the same carelessness, it would pose a serious problem for the entire world.
elected representatives from European overseas regions and territories composed entirely or almost entirely of islands, nearly 130.
In total, spread across the three continents, I must express here my deep concern regarding our islands in the face of offshore oil exploration projects.
This is why it is imperative that the European Union, as an example to follow, re-examines with the utmost rigor the safety and prevention measures on its own offshore oil platforms.
Moreover, accidental or operational marine oil spills, not necessarily attributable to Europe, are estimated at several tens of thousands of tonnes annually.

Would it not be appropriate for the European Union to take initiatives so that offshore platforms henceforth fall under an undisputed international scientific authority, which could be established in the form of an international body?
Following Copenhagen and in preparation for Cancún, the European Union would do well to initiate genuine global governance for the research, protection, and exploitation of deep-sea resources.

Building on the Barcelona process, the Union for the Mediterranean presents an opportunity for the development of our relations with the countries of the eastern and southern shores of the Mediterranean basin.
And as the excellent report by Vincent Péion rightly emphasizes, we cannot disregard either the potential of this region or the dangers it entails.
Yet, recognizing this clearly, since the bombardment of Gaza and the ongoing colonization of Palestinian territories, the Union for the Mediterranean is at an impasse.
The political aspect is stalled.
It is not even certain that the summit scheduled for next June in Barcelona will actually take place.
I would not be surprised if we learned in the coming hours or days that he has gone missing.
We must not therefore give up. But the European Union must take greater responsibility in seeking a political solution.
We cannot allow the United States to have a monopoly on the political management of the crisis in this region, which is ours, while limiting ourselves to contributing to its development.
The development and stability of the Mediterranean are essential to our security and growth, and for this reason, the Union for the Mediterranean remains the best instrument at our disposal.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this is an extremely important issue concerning consumer protection.

A recent survey indicates that Europeans perceive the food security situation as having worsened over the past ten years. And one in three consumers remains skeptical about the quality of food products.
Therefore, updating, simplifying, and clarifying food labeling across the European Union is an obligation for better consumer protection.
Allow me to address two points.
Regarding the color code, we support this code provided it is accompanied by a reference to nutritional values expressed as percentages.
Moreover, I am opposed to mandatory nutritional labeling for wines and spirituous beverages.
We wish to see the development of a specific regulation for wines and spirituous beverages within the next three years, rather than the five years proposed by the European Commission.
That is what I wished to point out, Mr. President, Commissioner.
Madam President, High Representative, this attack on civilian vessels by Israeli commandos undoubtedly requires an impartial and credible investigation.
But what credibility could we assign to a commission that would be both judge and party?
This attack occurred in international waters, and thus concerns the international community, particularly the European countries bordering the Mediterranean.
Europe, which should speak out more and engage more strongly in seeking a political solution based on two states, each with Jerusalem as its capital.

Europe is making considerable financial efforts to contribute to the development of Palestinian territories, but unfortunately, it is too absent from the political process that should lead to the establishment of a Palestinian state.
Our financial and budgetary goodwill is not sufficient
We must also express a political will and take the necessary measures to make ourselves heard.
The Israeli army's abrupt movement of the Mavi Marmara provokes emotion and condemnation.
emotion in the face of the nine victims of the civilian assault, condemnation because this boarding in international zones constitutes a violation of international law.
In doing so, the Israeli government penalizes its own people, as ultimately we see, with the strengthening of extremist factions, the ultimate goal of security in the region receding further.
Yes, more than ever, the government of Israel must abandon the policy of force.
I wish to point out, however, that the intense media coverage surrounding the Mavi Marmara flotilla, orchestrated from Turkey, casts doubt on the exclusively humanitarian nature of this operation.
An investigation appears indispensable to shed light on the assault, as well as on the preparations that preceded this expedition.
I wish for the Israeli government to accept that this investigation include substantial international participation, in order to finally restore the path to justice in this region. Thank you.
Madam President, High Representative, I welcome the new momentum generated by the launch of the partnership for modernization and its pragmatism.
This new partnership, even if it served no other purpose, would at least have the merit of highlighting just how indispensable Russia is as a geopolitical partner for the European Union.
I also welcome the positive remarks regarding cooperation with Russia on crisis management.
A few remarks, however.

While the requirements of the Union are consistently emphasized, the interests or positions of its partner appear systematically underestimated or disregarded, whether in the field of energy or in how it assesses the Eastern Partnership and the Atlanticist aspirations of its closest neighbors.

I also observe that the firmness displayed by most member states and the Union in their statements, as well as the severity of condemnations regarding human rights violations, appears to be directed exclusively at Russia.

The remarks are more restrained, diplomacy more muted, as if referring to China, for instance, regarding these systematic violations of the rule of law—let alone the issue of Laogaï—this global factory in light of minimal European social standards, especially when our French or European workers are unemployed.

Allow me to remind you of the maxim "Ad angusta per augusta" — "To the heights, through narrow paths" — for this is how I view Russia's current efforts, freed from the criminal poison of Marxism that for so many decades bled and imprisoned this country.
The current Russian Federation has managed to lift the head of an entire nation, despite the significant difficulties and challenges that still lie ahead.

So, a little humility, gentlemen of European integration, especially when the European super-state itself fails to achieve any of its noble objectives, despite increasingly costly resources and the growing dominance of the Western European and Central and Eastern European states.
I cannot help but recall that this assembly showed greater indulgence toward the Soviet Union than toward Russia.

Indeed, it is often the very same individuals who yesterday advocated for the unilateral disarmament of the West and for participation in Mr. Gorbachev's "common house," who now experience the most virtuous indignation and the greatest moral qualms regarding the Russia-EU partnership.

On this subject, I must inform you—should you not yet be aware—that new historical facts will emerge as a result of the progressive translation of the secret Soviet archives, which a courageous man, Pavel Stroilov, managed to obtain. Archives that have already yielded and will continue to yield numerous revelations.

unpleasant surprise for the orthodox doctrine regarding the reality of the end of the Cold War, which was indeed, Mr. Gorbachev, and for the betrayal by today's Europeanist powers, who at the time enthusiastically sought to make certain Soviet projects possible.
At the risk of repeating myself, Russia is an essential and strategic partner for European states, and our relations must be strengthened for the benefit of all parties.

This country is far closer to us in terms of culture, civilization, history, geography, mutual interest, or shared risk than are certain states you are nevertheless about to admit to the European Union.
Mr. President of the Council, it is often said that necessity makes law.
Facing the crisis, an obvious truth—indeed, an unavoidable necessity—has emerged: the need to innovate, to be creative.

In this regard, I welcome the step taken by the Council—a step I hope will ultimately prove to be a giant leap toward genuine economic governance, for let us not be mistaken: mere coordination of economic policies will remain insufficient.
Might we achieve a genuine budgetary federation, not imposed by circumstances, but because we have collectively decided upon it ourselves?
Let us return to the Council's decisions, which nonetheless please me.
Strengthening the preventive component of the Stability and Growth Pact is a positive development, but there can be no genuine budgetary discipline without sanctions for those who fail to comply.
They have been strengthened, but not yet clarified. This is regrettable.

Otherwise, I will express my satisfaction, among other things, regarding the decisions made, such as the much-discussed competitiveness assessment dashboard, since that is precisely what this is about—reconnecting with competitiveness and sustainable growth for all.
It still supports the project to introduce a banking tax, the acceptance of taxing financial transactions, and the acceleration of financial regulation reforms.
Mr. President of the Council, all these decisions and orientations seemed a few months ago, indeed even just a few weeks ago, unrealistic, illusory, or truly hypothetical.
You're right, it's true, it represents a profound cultural shift on the scale of Europe's incremental policy approach.
That said, it is now absolutely necessary that, in October, the States implement these guidelines in a very concrete manner.

It will now depend on the Council whether this step taken becomes a historic leap forward or merely an empty wish, something we will all bitterly regret, and which a majority of citizens will particularly have to endure on a daily basis. Thank you.

Mr. President, we have already discussed this matter during the Presidents' Conference. Many groups remain divided on the Gallo report, and clearly, Parliament as a whole—every group—still needs to discuss it, as there are contradictory points that the groups have been unable to resolve.
That is why we requested postponing the debate and vote on the Gallo report, namely on September 2, when we will have to see what the Conference of Presidents decides.
Therefore, it is a very simple matter.
Since the fruit is not yet ripe, let's give it the summer to ripen and discuss it in September.

Thank you, Madam President. I must admit I find it difficult to keep up with my colleagues, who so enjoy displaying their strength and claiming a monopoly on our legitimacy in comparison to the other institutions of the Union.
Unfortunately, their stance is often laughable.
But when the members of this house, through hard work and conviction, assert a well-reasoned position,
We defend what resembles a European general interest against rigid states, but the situation changes completely.
In this debate on European supervision, the Parliament has played this role.
Regarding the European Systemic Risk Board, of which I am the fictive rapporteur for my group, I have advocated, like rapporteur Ms. Goulard, an ambitious approach.
I have advocated mechanisms ensuring a certain level of efficiency, and I have emphasized the primacy of the European Central Bank.
Why?
Because the European Central Bank is indeed the only community institution within this framework, and because it has demonstrated its seriousness.
Out of Christian charity, I shall refrain from mentioning the names of all the institutions and those great figures who suddenly disappeared during the storm.
We can consider ourselves fortunate to have had at least one captain on board during the height of the crisis.
I shall therefore be reassured tomorrow to see macroprudential supervision anchored in a strong community institution, while microprudential supervision will necessarily remain fragmented.
We have waited so long; the European Systemic Risk Board must get to work as soon as possible.
Time will tell whether adjustments will be necessary, but it is essential to start from a solid foundation, as systemic risks affect the entire European and global banking sector. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President. The final agreement reached on the revision of the directives concerning industrial emissions is more than acceptable, and I commend the work of compromise that has been accomplished.

This final package incorporates the key elements of the position I have advocated, together with several of my colleagues on the Environment Committee, aiming to make this text an ambitious yet realistic piece of legislation.
First, maintaining a certain degree of flexibility for businesses and competent authorities in granting exemptions.
Next, I would like to welcome the new version of the ESN, so controversial and now limited to certain sectors, which appears much more achievable.

It is also important to emphasize that the agreement reached enables better alignment with the ETS directive and definitively removes from the text of the new directive the largely irrelevant notion of establishing a market for SO2 and NOX allowances.
Some reservations to bear in mind, however, particularly those concerning the ground.
Since the SOL Directive remains pending, this important issue will unfortunately be addressed only by those Member States that have their own legislation in place.
Tomorrow, Parliament must adopt the elements of the agreement reached following the trilogues.

Enormous effort has been made from all sides to avoid sending this text back for conciliation—a text that, I must emphasize, is of crucial importance both for industry and for European environmental policy.
Madam President, each day brings new information showing the extent of the ecological disaster caused in the Gulf of Mexico by the offshore drilling platform rupture.
But let us not forget that other disasters of this nature are occurring elsewhere.
I am thinking in particular about what is currently happening in Nigeria, as well as what has been occurring there for a long time already.

That is why, in addition to the moratorium on new drilling, I believe there is a need to promote stricter regulations regarding platform management—not only European rules, but also international ones.
as for the European Union. I believe it is necessary for it to proactively establish measures to combat this disaster, and I think it is obvious
thus, as Ms. Damanaki stated, all this underscores the need for a firm and resolute choice in favor of clean and renewable energies.
and the European Union should set an example in this regard.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, reopening negotiations with Mercosur at a time when the European Union is preparing to redefine its agricultural policy reveals a dangerous lack of coherence on the part of the Commission.
Europe already imports 500,000 tonnes of beef.
A new opening of our market will come at the expense of farmers in Europe's disadvantaged regions, without assurance of compliance with sanitary and social standards.
we cannot accept it.
Europe purchases 38 million tonnes of soy and animal feed.
These imports from Brazil and Argentina account for more than 19 million hectares.
They are concentrated in the hands of three companies: Cargill, ADM, Bunge.
Between 2001 and 2004, soybean production destroyed more than 1.2 million hectares of tropical and equatorial forests.
It was one of the primary causes of the increase in greenhouse gas emissions and the loss of biodiversity.
The European Parliament has decided to produce a report on the recovery of vegetable protein autonomy for animal feed.
Environmental-friendly agriculture and the fight against climate change dictate this choice.
These efforts will be rendered futile by this project for a bilateral agreement.
Once again, the European Commission sacrifices its agriculture and farmers in order to secure uncertain concessions for service companies.
These concessions will in no way benefit the peasant families of Mercosur, but only the interests of agro-industrial groups.
To be convinced, it is enough to read the Tyson group's report. An American company, a major player in the international meat market,
which announces having invested in Brazil to, quote, and this is in its report, "use this country as a platform for exporting to Europe."
Other companies, such as the Doux group, had already anticipated this approach by acquiring the Brazilian group Frango Soul in 1998.
Many poultry farmers, particularly in Brittany, had suffered from this operation.
Europe, without wishing to continue its policy of offshoring by intensifying its attacks
against the peasants. We must stop
Yes, Mr. President, many colleagues are concerned about the potential consequences for European agriculture resulting from the reopening of negotiations on an association agreement with Mercosur.
My group and I share these concerns.

It is indeed to be feared, and as a member of parliament representing an ultra-peripheral region, I have firsthand experience with banana agreements. It is therefore to be feared that an agreement with Mercosur would impose difficult costs on numerous European agricultural sectors.
This is why an impact assessment is absolutely necessary.
It is also feared that this agreement may further steer Mercosur agriculture too heavily toward a productive model, to the detriment of the small-scale farms that exist in these countries.

An equilibrium must therefore be struck so that this agreement does not turn Latin American and European continents into superpowers essentially grounded in the doctrine of mercantilism. Thank you, Mr. President. Thank you, Mr. Rowe.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would simply like to say that perhaps we did not properly set our agenda today.

If we allow free trade agreements to enter the territory of Europe, bringing with them products that do not respect either our social standards or our environmental standards, then the reform of the CAP will be pointless.
Very clearly, we cannot remain in a hypocrisy where, on one hand, we impose environmental, sanitary traceability, and safety requirements,
to our farmers, on the grounds that consumers must be protected, while on the other hand allowing products to enter our continent freely without complying with these standards.
The question underlying the negotiation with Mercosur is indeed that one.

And the way in which matters were initiated, regardless of what we are told about Parliament having been very well informed and about proceedings having taken place with the utmost transparency and clarity, does not lead us to view favorably a negotiation that takes place at a time when, in parallel, negotiations continue at the WTO,
On the grounds of consistency, consumer protection, and the defense of our farmers, I do not believe we should continue along this path.

Thank you, Madam President. I would also like to commend the work carried out by rapporteur José Bové, to acknowledge the Commissioner, and to echo the remarks made by Mr. Dess, who spoke before me regarding the objectives of this report.

I believe it is very important that we clearly keep in mind that in the debate we are having, we are addressing a problem related to price volatility in agriculture and especially to the fact that when prices rise,
They are quickly reflected at the distribution level. And when prices decrease at the production level, there is no corresponding reduction for consumers.
There is indeed an organizational problem within the supply chain, and there is indeed a problem regarding the distribution of what is called added value within the supply chain.

And I believe José Bové's report offers, on several points, perspectives that must be highlighted, and here I am truly addressing the Commission, in an effort to break out of this fundamentally negative spiral.
First of all, it is the organization of the supply chain and the balance of power within the supply chain.
what are the proposals we are making so that producers, brought together, organized, and having greater influence within the supply chain, can negotiate
That is the question you raised, Mr. Commissioner for Interprofessional Bodies, but it is also a question that will be addressed on the basis of the contractual framework that will be established.
And I argue, and this is stated in the report, that this contractual basis is a European framework.
One cannot allow each country to solve this problem on its own.
There must be a European framework.
It must be flexible, but it must exist.
It is a proposal, an inseparable path for me.
Secondly, of course, the issue of price transparency.
And on that point, I support the establishment of this observatory, which is absolutely necessary in order to inform consumers about the evolution of prices.
Therefore, I support the proposals that have been put forward, and I hope, as Mr. Desse said, that a majority will follow.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, today I wish to welcome this debate, which once again underscores the need for the Union to assess and appreciate its regional diversity.
At a time when negotiations are underway for the new framework programmes 2014–2020, I believe it is important to emphasize the specific arrangements that should benefit specific regions.
Questions of territorial continuity and cohesion must be at the center of our concerns.
However, the development of mountainous, insular, or sparsely populated regions requires more than just a set of derogatory measures.
We need a horizontal approach
We must establish the instruments capable of synergistically integrating all areas of community action in order to strengthen their respective added values.
Moreover, as a representative of the outermost regions, I am eagerly awaiting the new EU strategy for the OMRs, which the Commission's services promised us for early 2011.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, first of all, I align myself with my colleague's position regarding the absence of the Council from this Parliament meeting.
I find that a bit careless on the part of the Council, and I want to say that as well.
As the next summit approaches on October 6, China and the European Union must absolutely and resolutely endeavor together to make progress on the issue of climate change.
Last year, at the same time, we were already engaged in this very hemicycle, urging Europe to do everything possible to achieve an ambitious and binding agreement in Copenhagen.
It has been observed that the outcome did not meet our expectations and ambitions.
Thus, concerns one year later are the same, or even much more intense.

In order to be able to compel or persuade our partners today, we must demonstrate that our determination remains absolutely intact, for example by ensuring that we provide the resources necessary to fulfill our commitments to Southern countries.
To this end, the establishment of innovative financing mechanisms becomes a necessary imperative. I heard President Sarkozy's speech at the UN yesterday.
For example, the introduction of a carbon border tax is not ruled out.

It would act as an incentive mechanism for countries that are not going far enough in their climate commitments, and far more than a mere threat. And a means of protecting our jobs and businesses, particularly to better address the post-crisis period.

Thank you, Madam President. For me, the need to enhance family leave is beyond dispute. Raising the minimum threshold for maternity leave is a step forward, a benefit, and we should not be entirely demagogic in comparing its economic impact to the qualitative benefits, which are difficult to quantify.

But the problem is essentially twofold. 1. The economic context, indeed, but insufficient to leave millions of families stranded for decades. 2. The legal gaps in the report, as the text contains several types of family leave whose legal foundations are incompatible.
Let us take the adoption leave provided for in the text, treating it on the same footing as maternity and paternity leave.
Personally, as an adoptive mother, and on behalf of all those I represent, I value the desire to grant rights equivalent to those of biological mothers.
I am in fact the additional small package referred to by Mrs. Morin, appended to the Estrella report.

If the objective is genuinely to strengthen the health and rights of women, of all women, in the labor market, then adoptive mothers, having become mothers in the same way as other mothers, are entitled to the same rights and the same protection at work.
They are mothers just like any other; indeed, she adopts a child who is either less than or more than 12 months old—no discrimination, please, as stated in the text
Regarding adoption, I regret that the text was not explored in greater depth.
It was not even informed by the Rambol impact study.
All of this is not well developed, which is clearly a weakness.

Nevertheless, despite these reservations, I will support Mrs. Estrella's report, because beyond economic considerations, there are men and women who must not only assume their parental responsibilities more fully in a society of

increasingly disengaged from the education of these young people, but also because it is our duty to ensure that no one has to choose between sacrificing their children for their work or sacrificing their work for their children.
Finally, we do not sit on the Council but in Parliament. If we, directly elected representatives, are not ambitious, tell me who will be.
Thank you, Madam President, Commissioner, Mr. President of the Council. I would like to speak, without going back over the debate as it has unfolded so far.

We discussed the 2011 budget under the constraints we are aware of, namely those arising from the deep crisis currently affecting Europe, along with national public deficits, which may lead to two hypotheses.
These national public deficits constrain the European deficit, where these national deficits must provide a new direction and renewed momentum at the European level.
That is precisely the question.
And I ask you, Mr. Commissioner, above all to try to think, when discussing a budgetary revision, when we begin to talk about budgetary perspectives beyond 2013, not to...
remain completely immersed in the context of the crisis. Unless you tell me, and tell all the citizens of Europe, that Europe will indeed remain in crisis for a long time.
Our entire challenge is indeed to manage the crisis and reduce our deficits and debt. But it is also about preparing for exit from the crisis.
And it is precisely here that the budgetary question arises.

We cannot overcome the crisis—this was immediately stated by Joseph Dehôde—unless the European budget, because it is European, can provide us with the means to regain what we lack today: growth, job creation, innovation, and research.

That is the issue at stake. And it is from this perspective that we must discuss the budget—not continually, as we too often do, bringing in the questions of crisis and deficits. And here, the Council is excellent.

I noted that in Deauville, Angela Merkel and Nicolas Sarkozy even announced in advance a reform of the treaties, without having discussed anything at all with Mr. Van Rompuy, in order to sanction all states that fail to respect budgetary discipline tomorrow.

This is where we stand today with regard to the Council, while Parliament is asking for only one thing—particularly of the Commission—to try to advance the European project. And this happens through the budget.
Then you noted a number of points on which we entirely agree.
Greater flexibility is needed.
Of course we must find together a way to have a much more flexible budget. Because it's a matter of necessity—the responsiveness of Europe is at stake.
It also entails, of course, establishing priorities and organizing the efficiency of the European budget.
We will all agree on this. All of us. It is a necessity.
But where we must be clear with one another is that we need a schedule. We must jointly set objectives, Commissioner. It cannot remain merely words, mere commitments, mere

texts that are, moreover, very interesting and which I nearly fully share. But now, we must get down to concrete matters. In particular, on one essential issue around which we keep circling: own resources.

How do we secure our own resources for Europe tomorrow? Not just for Europe, but for all European citizens. And how do we collectively regain the path toward growth, and a stronger path still for Europe tomorrow?
It's your turn, Mr. Commissioner.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to focus my remarks on the preparation for the upcoming G20 summits.

Everything has already been said by previous speakers regarding European economic governance, on the need to strengthen European economic governance, to reinforce the Stability and Growth Pact, and to involve the European Parliament and, of course, also national parliaments more effectively in the process.
I would like to briefly mention two challenges facing our nations and the members of the G20, which, in my view, must be addressed within the framework of the upcoming G20 meetings.
the challenge of currency wars and that of commodity price volatility.
I believe that on these two issues, which constitute genuine threats to global growth and cause very significant imbalances across our planet, the European Union must...

manage to organize themselves, establish common positions, and equip themselves to speak with one voice at the upcoming G20 summits in Seoul, as well as those that will follow, and more broadly on the global stage. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. Dear colleagues, I would first like to congratulate our colleague Diogo Feio on the quality of the report and on the ambitious proposal it contains.
Thus, it also demonstrates that the European Parliament is fully asserting its role in a debate that is crucial for Europe's future, and we can only welcome this development.
Moreover, the Greek crisis served as a revealing indicator of the dysfunctional elements undermining the economic governance of the European Union.
I therefore note Mr. Feillot's proposal to establish a permanent mechanism for financial stability.
We must also address the problem at its root.
To this end, I believe the solution also lies in strengthening the Stability and Growth Pact, and in particular its sanctions.
This is essential if we are to achieve a sustainable improvement in the budgetary situation of states, which is not always popular,
It is burdensome, but we have no choice.

I believe the report clearly highlights the challenges posed by the marine environment in terms of fishing, aquaculture, transportation, tourism, energy, mineral and biological resources, and research, all combined with climate change.
It is a considerable development potential that must be secured, protected, and sustainably enhanced, as humanity's future depends on it.
The report also rightly notes that the European Union is the world's leading maritime power and that its outermost regions provide it with the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.
given such a situation, it is therefore up to the European Union to implement an ambitious policy, a major maritime policy.
If it is not the world's foremost maritime power doing it, who else will take its place?
But it must be acknowledged that the resolution does not go very far in this direction.
One cannot claim leadership in global politics with 50 million euros.
Finally, although the outermost regions have a recognized strategic position, given their presence in all oceans, a change could have been made.
It was judicious to involve them in a major ocean strategy, a point unfortunately not retained in the report, and I regret that. Thank you, Mr. President.
Madam, Commissioner, I would like to return to the anti-counterfeiting agreement currently being negotiated, particularly with the United States.
This agreement also raises questions in the United States, particularly among officials at the United States Patent Office.
Moreover, 75 American law professors have just sent a letter to President Obama.
He considers that ACTA, being merely an executive agreement, would be contrary to the United States Constitution.
While, as you know, the European Parliament is preparing to adopt a resolution on the ACTA agreement, which continues to raise major concerns and is far from being universally supported.
Among our fellow citizens, I believe it would be helpful to receive a very clear response from our American counterparts. On two points, can ACTA be ratified by the United States?
Is it, in their eyes, a binding or a voluntary agreement?
Indeed, an anti-counterfeiting agreement from which China, India, and Brazil are already absent, and which would not be implemented by the United States, would lose much of its credibility.
Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President. Throughout this difficult process of adopting the alternative investment funds directive, I have consistently advocated for the principle of equal treatment between fund managers, regardless of whether they are based in the European Union or elsewhere.
This principle sounds like an obvious truth, but this obviousness has encountered countless difficulties.
The result presented to us today is of a satisfactory nature.
I thank the negotiators, and above all our rapporteur, Mr. Gauzès, who, following this particularly painful experience for him, certainly deserves a substantial maternity leave.
I would especially like to emphasize that the issue of treating third countries must be approached from two perspectives, whether one is inside or outside the European Union.
Thanks in particular to the European Parliament, all funds marketed within the European Union will effectively be subject to comparable regulation.
One can thus speak, to a greater or lesser extent, of a "level playing field,"

It remains that during the transitional period, non-EU entities will benefit from the national placement regime, while EU funds and managers will be subject to the directive, which is generally stricter.
The situation is different on markets located outside the European Union. There, European funds and their managers will remain subject to the directive, whereas funds from third countries will not.
It will therefore be much more difficult to sell European products around the rest of the world, as they will be less flexible and more culturally specific.
We must be aware of this disadvantage, even though it was partly unavoidable.

Regarding custodians, let us also recognize that we will make the process more demanding, particularly due to the new responsibilities they will assume. Their involvement should lead to substantial changes in the profession.

Mr. President, I am not saying this to demonstrate my opposition, but to emphasize that this new regulation is by no means trivial. Its implementation, which is necessary, will also entail difficulties that we must be aware of.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, particularly at the beginning of the 21st century, patients need appropriate information in order to be fully involved in their care pathway, to know where to seek care, to decide on the most suitable treatment in agreement with healthcare professionals, and to strictly follow the prescribed treatment.
Today, patients demand independent, comparative, and personalized information tailored to their individual needs.
Unfortunately, the revision proposals for the directive submitted by the European Commission did not adequately address the needs expressed by European citizens.
These proposals opened the door to direct promotion by pharmaceutical companies, which appears to us as nonsensical in terms of addressing patients' actual needs.
This operation holds no interest for either citizens or Member States,
On the contrary, it meant additional bureaucracy, increased expenses, and putting patients at risk.
For these reasons, we had initially proposed rejecting these texts, thereby requesting the Commission to revise its draft

This approach, also adopted by my colleague Karl Schilter from the Greens group, has enabled our rapporteur, Mr. Fechner, whom I salute, to carry out a significant revision of the Commission's proposals.

At the same time, we had submitted around sixty amendments aimed at further protecting the health of our citizens and facilitating patients’ access to independent, comparative information tailored to individual needs.

The adoption of these amendments in the Environment Committee enabled greater emphasis to be placed on patients' rights to information, rather than making the provision of information an option for the pharmaceutical industry.
Given the compromise amendments achieved, we have decided to withdraw our proposal to reject the texts, as the substance of the text has evolved in a positive direction over the past few months.
The draft report as amended has managed to offset the European Commission's initial proposals.

However, we will continue to oppose the use of healthcare professionals to distribute to their patients brochures and information provided by pharmaceutical companies without any oversight by health authorities.
Likewise, we will oppose the authorization of industrial information campaigns, particularly with regard to vaccination.
Information cannot freely come from pharmaceutical companies.

Experience shows that we must be extremely vigilant, and indeed, I conclude, what happened with the Mediator medications demonstrates just how vigilant we must be and how essential it is to maintain that balance we have sought with this report proposal.

Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam Minister. Unity and realism will be reflected, at least I hope so, in tomorrow’s vote on our resolution regarding the 16th Conference of the Parties on climate change.

I therefore hope that our commissioner in charge will be able to pursue the practical objectives we collectively need to achieve, as well as defend the essential conditions set by Europe that are associated with them.
In these negotiations, we must likewise not lose sight of the legitimate expectations of fairness held by our fellow citizens and our industries.
It is the world that must combat climate change, not Europe alone.
Thus, recognizing the importance of intellectual property that is insufficiently protected will be essential.
Green technologies must benefit the greatest number, but we must not thereby undervalue our existing and future knowledge, even in this indispensable fight against climate change.

Similarly, requesting common calculation methods for emissions, along with verification and publication of data, so that all emitting industries are subject to the same standard, appears to be a perfectly legitimate request, not a negotiating tactic.
Finally, more controversial—and my colleague raised this point earlier—gaps in the current system of clean development mechanisms must also be addressed.
Dear colleagues, we are all fully aware, with regret, that the true negotiating power does not lie with the European Parliament.

But the determination of many European parliamentarians, including myself, to work toward and contribute to a positive outcome of this conference must be encouraged, rather than ignored, as we have felt in recent days.
If Europe wishes to be strong within international dialogues, its legislators must likewise be strong.
Thank you, Madam President. The European Union often presents economic partnership agreements as an opportunity for African countries.
She therefore despairing of not seeing these agreements signed as soon as possible?
But the question that concerns us is why these countries do not rush to sign agreements that would be so favorable to them.
Clearly, opinions differ regarding the quality of these agreements. As long as these differences in assessment persist, ACP countries will be reluctant to sign them.
Would it not be appropriate to go further in dialogue and understanding by listening to the concerns and expectations of the ACP countries?
They wish to discuss development strategy, whereas Europe often responds only with market opening.
It is this listening that the ACP-EU Parliamentary Assembly imposes upon itself.
I believe other European bodies should adopt the same approach if we are to establish a genuine strategic partnership between Africa and the European Union.
Thank you. Christians are undoubtedly one of the most persecuted religious groups in the world.

In the Middle East, they are perceived by certain extremist groups as, and I quote, "legitimate targets." The objective is their outright departure from this region— a region that gave birth to Christianity and where their historical presence is essential to a certain balance.
The events at the cathedral in Baghdad are merely the culmination of a situation we have passively observed for far too long.
The European Union, as a global actor and commercial partner of many countries in this region, must strongly express its indignation in light of the situation.
Discrimination of any kind cannot be tolerated.
Democracy will prevail in this region only when fundamental human rights, such as religious freedom, are respected.
My colleagues, we are all deeply concerned by the serious crisis currently taking place in Côte d'Ivoire.

It is clear that the decision by this country's Constitutional Council to challenge, in violation of the electoral code, the results of elections officially proclaimed by the Independent Electoral Commission has been unanimously condemned by the international community.

The European Union, the United Nations, the African Union, the Parity Parliamentary Assembly, the EU-ACP Union, the Co-As, and soon certainly the European Parliament, are demanding respect for the official proclamation.

election results and the installation of the new President of the Republic, as recognized by the Independent Electoral Commission. The Independent Electoral Commission, which I remind you, is an institution validated by the United Nations.
All political groups in the European Parliament have expressed, in a joint statement, their agreement on these essential points.

They also called for halting any escalation of tensions in the country that could lead to civil war, which would be a human, social, and economic disaster for Côte d'Ivoire—something that must obviously be avoided at all costs.

Thank you, Madam President. I would like to thank our four rapporteurs in turn for their outstanding work and for significantly enhancing the European Parliament’s contribution to the texts initially proposed by the Commission.
The citizens' initiative that we, in the PPE, are about to vote for enthusiastically is a major advancement of the Lisbon Treaty.
Let us not be deceived: this instrument can truly transform the political landscape of Europe. Provided it is used with both responsibility and daring. I repeat—responsibility and daring.
It can help bring European citizens directly into the European political debate.

It can help foster a European public opinion, that is, men and women who are citizens of Europe, engaging in joint debate, taking collective positions, and coming together on issues genuinely European in nature.
But let us not disappoint our fellow citizens.
Let us urge our member states to take swift implementing measures where necessary. Let us not disappoint our fellow citizens; this is essential for strengthening European democracy. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. I, too, would like to address, like several of my colleagues, the issue of the persecution of Christians throughout the world, particularly Christians of the East.
We do not need to recall the horrible acts recently committed.
I would like to emphasize this point just as strongly as defending Christians in the East and everywhere in the world, advocating for religious freedom, the diversity of
It means advocating for freedom, democracy, and human rights—values at the heart of the European project, which are, of course, universally recognized, and we are all convinced of this here.
It is truly time now, beyond outrage, emotion, and solemn condemnations—undoubtedly necessary—truly time now to move from words to action.

And thus I urgently call upon the authorities of the European Union, the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, as well as all our policymakers, the ministers of foreign affairs,
undertake precise measures, implement a clear, concrete action plan, to make respecting religious diversity and combating all forms of religious persecution a priority. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, High Representative, we all agree that the situation in the Sahel region has significantly deteriorated.
And allowing a lawless zone to take root at Europe's doorstep is a danger we must resolutely and immediately confront.
Extremist groups are multiplying and threatening the lives of civilian populations.
In my turn, I deplore the loss of human life and hostage takings, and I am concerned about European nationals present on the ground.
To date, Morocco has served as a barrier against all kinds of trafficking originating from the Sahel.
Nowadays, drug, arms, and human traffickers bypass Morocco by passing through Mauritania to the Canary Islands, en route to the European Union.
It seems to me that it is the responsibility of the European Union to act in order to assist these states in putting an end to these threats.
Europe's security is played out on our territory, as well as in its immediate neighborhood.
It is time for us to act, and I call on the Commission and the Council to implement an action plan to help restore security in the Sahel.
I join the expressions of sympathy and support already extended to the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt.
It represents a symbol of hope for all defenders of freedom.
A thought as well for the victims.

I would now like to say that the European Union, which, as has been noted, has been criticized for its lack of response, must stand firmly alongside the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt to help them successfully open their countries to reform and establish democracy.

The European Union has decided to support Tunisian leaders in launching a peaceful transition, preparing the organization of upcoming elections to secure freedom, and enabling the Tunisian people to achieve prosperity through development and social peace, creating an economy that will provide employment opportunities for youth.

To conclude, I would say that the Union for the Mediterranean faces a significant challenge, as the Jasmine Revolution has triggered a wave across all the states of the Maghreb, the Middle East, and the Near East.
It must implement a robust strategy toward these countries.

But beware, in the face of this legitimate democratic movement, Europe must strike a balance between non-interference in internal affairs, support for the legitimate aspirations of peoples, and stability.
Commissioner, my colleagues have reminded him of this on numerous occasions,

At a time when Europe is reflecting on a common strategy for its raw material supply, and particularly for rare earths, we cannot overlook the immense potential represented by the intelligent valorization of our waste.

Strengthen European standards to improve the functioning of collection chains. Address issues related to leakage or illegal export of such materials. Or, increase our collection targets, but these measures must also be integrated into this legislation.
Let us not forget the main actor in the success or failure of this regulation: the consumer.
Citizen accountability is crucial. If we wish to implement effective legislation,
And to this end, my dear Karl Heinz, the eco-contribution has proven to be one of the most relevant tools, among other systems.

Electrical and electronic equipment producers must obviously take greater involvement in collection and recycling systems, but citizens cannot be exempted from responsibility for the waste they generate.
Our citizens must be actors, not spectators, of European environmental policy.

Thank you, Madam President, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. I would like to begin by also sharing the observation made just now by Madame McGuinness regarding the essential link between food issues and agricultural issues.
The issue that concerns us is the rise in food prices, along with the consequences it may have on a number of populations around the world, as well as in Europe.
I would first like to try to share an observation.

The trend toward rising agricultural prices and agricultural commodity prices is, in my view, a long-term trend that will persist for three fundamental reasons. The world's demographic evolution and the increase in prices.

of the world's population. Second point: the fact that, with climate change, we have entered a period of climatic hazards that pose serious problems, whether droughts or floods. And the third point, because the world is also developing
With new emerging countries, consumption levels are increasing.
Therefore, the pressure on agricultural markets and agricultural raw materials is a major trend.
It is exacerbated by price volatility. And price volatility is a consequence of the financialization of the global economy.
And it is on this very issue that we must also focus, if we are to address both the agricultural question and the food question.

And to achieve this, what the Commissioner has said is important. There are two elements. Yes, there must be transparency regarding stockpiles. But there must also be a public policy on stockpiles, both in Europe and a global management of stockpiles, particularly at the United Nations level.

And secondly, financial markets and financial speculation must be regulated, particularly by ensuring that speculative volumes no longer have any connection whatsoever with the physical quantities traded on these markets.
We cannot accept that, on the issue of food, speculators are allowed to speculate without regard for the consequences, since this concerns the food of the world and humanity. And I conclude by saying
that leads me to two major consequences. We need a strong European policy, and we reaffirm strategically Europe's commitment to being a global player in food and agriculture.

Secondly, we need to reform our development policy so that, on a global scale—Europe being an actor in this process—we once again invest in agriculture, because we will need all forms of agriculture to feed the world.
Madam President, Commissioner, As in 2008, agricultural commodity prices are rising on global markets,
We are only at 3% of the 2008 level.
The decline in production in certain traditional exporting regions due to climate change, the diversion of cereals toward biofuel production, and speculation
Financial factors that have exacerbated upward pressures are responsible for this situation, as shown by reports from the IMF, French authorities, and also stated by the Commission.
The consequences of this new food crisis are severely affecting the 1.2 billion people worldwide who live below $1.5 per day.
Since October, an additional 44 million people have joined those living below the poverty line.
Two days before the G20 meeting, what is Europe waiting for to take similar measures to those passed in the United States?
Since January 13, 2011, the U.S. administration has required the limitation and transparency of positions taken by financial funds in agricultural commodity markets.
It is taking the first step toward countering the appetites of speculators.
The impact is also dramatic for European farmers.
they can no longer feed their animals
Hundreds of farms are bankrupt.
Five years ago, wheat was worth 100 euros per tonne. Today, it is trading at nearly 300 euros.
The situation has therefore completely changed.
The Commission demands that farmers adapt to market signals. It would do well today to follow its own advice.
Faced with this crisis, the European Union cannot wait until 2014 to find solutions.
We must confront the crisis, and I propose three concrete actions.
First, the creation of grain stocks in cereal-producing countries to make feed available to farmers at subsidized prices immediately.
Second, indexing the prices of meat paid to farmers to the fluctuations in livestock feed prices.
Thirdly, for 2011, a mechanism to transfer a portion of the subsidies intended for cereal support to livestock production. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. Are we ready in Europe to face a pandemic?
The answer is yes, but there is a caveat
The 2009–2010 H1N1 influenza episode showed that each member state reacted on its own behalf, without significant coherence or even solidarity.

As the fictitious rapporteur on this file, I believe it was necessary to make these observations, but above all to go further, to be constructive and concrete for our citizens, who, we must admit, and this seems to me the most regrettable aspect, have likely lost some confidence in public health messages.

Ms. Rivasi's report, which will be voted on tomorrow, takes into account these fundamental priorities, including the systematic strengthening of cooperation among Member States, and better coordination with the
European health institutions, clarification of the roles of European health risk management bodies, and an assessment of vaccination and communication strategies implemented by member states.
It is therefore a good report; I congratulate the rapporteur. Naturally, it must not remain a dead letter.
It is the responsibility of our member states, which retain the majority of the competences in this area. It is therefore a genuine responsibility to be upheld towards our citizens.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would now like to express my profound emotion following the cowardly assassination on 2 March of Mr. Bhatti, the Pakistani minister responsible for Christian minority affairs.
This terrorist-style assassination follows numerous others targeting courageous, tolerant Pakistani individuals—human rights defenders and advocates for women's rights, I must say.

Once again, we urgently call on the Pakistani government to shed full light on this cowardly assassination and to effectively, truly effectively, protect those threatened by fanatical religious extremists.
And of course, above all else, though not exclusively, I think of Mrs. Sherry Raymond.
And I would also, once again, strongly urge all competent institutions of the European Union to include in the negotiations aimed at
prepare future cooperation agreements, in particular the cooperation agreement between the EU and Pakistan, including a clause on respect for religious freedom and human rights. Thank you.
Mr. President, for the record, due to the crisis and the bursting of the real estate and credit bubbles, the Irish State was forced to intervene to support the banks.
Ireland's public deficit and the cumulative budget balance of central, local, and particularly social security administrations was estimated at 32% of GDP in 2010.

If the Irish government, which finds it difficult to refinance itself on financial markets, wishes to obtain loans from the Mechanism and the European Financial Stability Facility, it will have to comply with obligations involving social and fiscal reforms, even though these areas fall under the principle of subsidiarity.
In our view, this is not contradictory.
As a physician, I would say that in cases of fever, it is pointless to prescribe antipyretics blindly without seeking to treat the underlying cause.
In the event of the allocation of these funds, it is up to that State to restore the soundness of its finances in order to eliminate its excessive deficit and rectify its previous errors.
This austerity plan is expected to improve GDP by 10% over the next four years.

Just as an individual bank, without interfering in private life or personal management, would condition a personal loan on proof of solvency, likewise the European Union may lend to Ireland based on consolidated solvency.
Without interfering in its private affairs, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a salary increase or to borrow elsewhere.

Thus, respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, based on a memorandum established with the IMF, considers that this solvency requires reducing the minimum wage and adjusting pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
the decision to implement these measures lies with Ireland.

It is not a free pass for European Union interference in areas of subsidiarity, which could, for example, dangerously impose a European minimum wage level, but rather a safeguard for the stability of the eurozone through a process of mutual assurance.

Of course, we should assist Ireland as we do other countries facing difficulties, as this also protects us; but not at any price, particularly not at the cost of allowing a state unable to repay its debts in the long term, thereby weakening its citizens and those across the entire European Union.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us just said, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and inefficiency of European airspace.
And each of us here in this chamber has been directly affected.
and five million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded without a viable alternative means of transportation.
And a year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you mentioned, Mr. Commissioner, relating to volcanic ash, is duly scheduled for the 13th and 14th of April next year, under the coordination of Eurocontrol, ICAO, and IATA; however, it is essential that the AESA assume real leadership in ensuring the coordination of safety measures at the European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the London and Toulouse VACs must be refined using more modern technological means.
These data, as seen last April, may however be contradictory and may lead each Member State to decide on closing its airspace, less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of the AESA is essential in providing a harmonized interpretation of these data, as well as ensuring the centralization and effective dissemination of information.
Beyond the information provided by VAC, the AESA must establish a data collection mechanism by closely involving the Member States, airlines, and their pilots.
This centralization of information and coordination of all involved parties will help prevent the chaos and inconsistencies we witnessed last year.
I therefore expect the Commission, in collaboration with AESA, to swiftly present concrete proposals that will be fully integrated into the implementation of the European Sky.
President, dear colleagues, this text is important.
This is not a revolution. One cannot claim that everything was bad before and everything is perfect now.
Nevertheless, it presents a number of significant advancements at this provisional stage of our work.

In my opinion, the main issues concern improved oversight of distance sales, and in particular those conducted via the Internet. This is addressed in Chapter 3, as consumer rights in this area need to be strengthened.
E-commerce is highly beneficial. It broadens consumer choice, enables consumers to obtain the lowest prices, and also allows small and medium-sized enterprises and craftsmen to access new markets.

To make progress in this area, it was necessary to do so in a genuinely European manner, that is, by focusing on a few specific points and establishing a comprehensive harmonization of everyone's rights and obligations.
Very concretely, it was essential that an Italian customer purchasing on a Belgian or German website now be aware that they will enjoy the same rights and the same protection.
This is precisely what this directive will make possible.
This means that its withdrawal period will be extended to 14 days throughout the Union.
This means he will immediately know the total amount he has to pay, without fear of an unpleasant surprise afterward.
This means that by double-clicking, he will properly verify his order.
Of course, all this will disrupt certain habits.
In many states, there are subtleties in national law that everyone considers fundamental, even though they are sometimes nowhere to be found in neighboring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to slightly adjust one's habits in the service of the common and general interest.
Let us be clear: this text will still need to be improved, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks, in collaboration with the Council and the Commission.
And I have appreciated the openings that have just been made in this regard.
But we have already carried out substantial work, thanks to our rapporteur, Andréa Schwab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real outcome of this work is that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all political groups.
Consumer confidence in the EU's internal market is very fragile.
This political unanimity was necessary to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.
At point number 7, after the first paragraph, a first dash: ensuring respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular the freedom of the individual.
Freedom of speech and assembly, the right to peacefully demonstrate, the obligation to listen to and address the legitimate demands of demonstrators, and ensuring their safety.

Thank you, Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner. The Open Skies agreement between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only that—a step that places Europe at a crossroads.
The transport committee and the committee itself have emphasized this point.
Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European companies, or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits gained since 2008.
Let me then draw a parallel with private and professional pilot licenses, a text that will be discussed before summer. For the pilots to whom I belong, this creates a dilemma.
The European Union Aviation Safety Agency, wishing to harmonize licenses across the European territory, will do so at the expense of the 80,000 to 90,000 general aviation pilots
directly involved, some holding only a single U.S. license, and to the detriment, which is crucial, of businesses and jobs associated with this activity.
I would like to draw your attention to this project, which lacks a security foundation.
There are 31 times fewer accidents among holders of American licenses.

The objectives of the foundational regulation text 216-2008 allow for the recognition and equivalence of licenses whose benefits do not justify waiting for hypothetical agreements postponed until 2014.
Thus, I propose an open sky agreement and even invite you to accompany me when I occasionally come to Parliament, personally piloting to demonstrate that an American pilot's license is entirely safe.

It would be logical for groups to submit a quotation in advance to the Visits Service, receive a deposit from Parliament paid into a bank account, and then have the balance paid upon presentation of actually incurred invoices, of course subject to a ceiling. Thank you.
Thank you, Madam President. I am addressing the Commission, my colleagues, and I welcome the presence of the Council.
The question arising today stems from the management of the Schengen area in 2011.

This is no longer the space of the five Member States of the 1980s; on the contrary, it has steadily expanded in recent years and now comprises 25 states, three of which are not members of the European Union.
Like all European policies, the Schengen cooperation must therefore adapt to the reality it currently reflects, which is no longer the same as it was ten years ago.

It is in this sense that one must interpret the proposals put forward by the Italian and French governments and the pragmatic and necessary responses provided today by the Commission in its communication on immigration.
Mutual trust among the States parties to the Schengen area is undoubtedly the cornerstone of Schengen cooperation.
But this trust is based on mutual obligations.
If one of the parties fails to meet its obligations and creates air leaks, the entire system becomes dysfunctional.
It must therefore be possible for other states to address this failure by reestablishing temporary and strictly limited control over these borders.
In doing so, these failing states will be clearly encouraged to improve their control over their external borders.

Moreover, as presented by the Commission, these measures would fully respect the spirit of Schengen, inasmuch as such controls would be reintroduced only as a last resort and the decision would be made at the European level, not on an intergovernmental basis.
Such measures will be inherently linked to the broader range of measures envisaged in the long term, within the framework of a common migration policy.
It is particularly essential to strengthen Frontex, to provide greater solidarity, I conclude, in support of the Member States located at the external borders.
The situation experienced by the Mediterranean in recent years merely underscores a shortcoming of the Kishengen.
Just two more minutes, Madam President. In just a few weeks, migrants coming from countries bordering the Mediterranean have accounted for a significant proportion of annual French immigration.
This achievement is not static, but must instead evolve.
I hope these proposals will be swiftly adopted in order to ensure the public a better management of our Schengen area and, thereby, guarantee the security of our common external borders. Thank you.

and we shall all assess in this hemicycle the impact of such a decision on the affected citizens.
