Thank you, Mr. President. In its Europe 2020 strategy, the European Union has set itself the objective of making our economy greener and our transport systems more modern.
All European institutions have strongly supported this objective, including the vast majority of Member States and our Parliament.
In this context, it is therefore regrettable that since 2008, several Member States have sought to undermine the substance of the new eurovignette directive proposal and have blocked this file.

Today, however, thanks to the perseverance of our rapporteur, and I would like to salute Saïd El Khadraoui here, and thanks to the perseverance of the shadow rapporteurs in their negotiations with the Council, we finally have a compromise before us.

Certainly, this text is considerably less ambitious than the initial proposal, but it will for the first time allow the polluter-pays principle to be put into practice and enable the internalization of external costs of transport, such as noise or CO2 emissions.

For this eurovignette to be more than just a tax and truly contribute to improving transport across Europe, Member States are also encouraged to reinvest the collected funds in modernizing transport modes.
The Parliament has also succeeded in securing concrete advancements regarding the interoperability of toll systems, as well as stronger incentives for the use of less polluting heavy goods vehicles.

In order to remain consistent with the political objectives set by our institution, I sincerely hope that we will be able to adopt the compromise proposed to us at second reading and thus pave the way for a more sustainable transport policy. Thank you.

toward a discipline that has been so lacking for us, a step, I say, nothing more, which will demonstrate its usefulness in the future.

This report outlines an agency with its capabilities multiplied tenfold, better aligned with the missions assigned to it.

Frontex finally has the means to carry out its missions.

I approve the possibility of exchanging personal data with other European agencies, in particular Europol.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr. Meyer, as well as the sometimes critical remarks from my colleagues. But this 2010 activity report finally provides a splendid opportunity to discuss the Petitions Committee, as it remains too little known.

Madam President, Commissioner, I welcome this resolution on homelessness, as it is our responsibility to underscore its extreme urgency.
We first observe that what was a priority yesterday is even more pressing today.

The lack of a definition and reliable statistical indicators on homelessness cannot prevent us from observing, aided by the crisis, that the number of people living below the poverty threshold rose, according to Eurostat, from 79 million in 2009 to an estimated 84 million in 2011.
Dear colleagues, three years ago, our Parliament, concerned with eradicating poverty, called on Member States to eliminate the issue of food insecurity by 2015.
Today, we ourselves are somewhat closer to our goal.

Was the 2010 International Year for the Decade of Action to Combat Poverty genuinely utilized by Member States to address concretely this profound precariousness, the urgency of which has become permanent?
I genuinely doubt it, and I deeply regret it.

To conclude, I wish to emphasize the need to develop a comprehensive, multisectoral strategy on homelessness, as clearly there is poverty and a lack of housing, certainly, but there are also family issues, health concerns, various addictions, and personal vulnerabilities.
A comprehensive, effective, ambitious, yet tailored approach is required—one that takes into account individuals, the heterogeneity of their trajectories, and their life ruptures.
It is a fundamental right that many of the most vulnerable precarious individuals are deprived of.
Thank you, Madam President. As everyone has said in recent years, the European Union has been struck by unprecedented disasters. And the cities here would take up the entire duration of the debate.

Thank you, Mr. President. Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Minister, the number of poor and unemployed people continues to rise within the European Union.

Today, six member states wish to terminate the food aid programme without any replacement solution. Germany, Sweden, Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and the Czech Republic.

However, Mr. Commissioner, the Parliament questioned you last July on the initiative of our group, the Greens, and you made relevant proposals to safeguard the programme.
The Greens are ready to work with you on long-term solutions. But once millions of people depend on this system, we cannot allow the food aid budget to be cut by a factor of four.
The urgency is real.

If nothing is done by the end of the year, food banks will face a shortfall of more than 300 million euros, and there is no guarantee that national governments will be able to step in to fill the gap.
It is therefore a genuine question of solidarity among peoples.
Let us not allow a minority of irresponsible governments to deprive millions of Europeans of food aid, as this is above all a matter of survival.

Come to your senses. Do we need food riots in Europe to realize the pressing need to change our approach?

When it comes to saving banks, the Council has always found solutions; but when it comes to saving food banks, suddenly no one is around. The Parliament made a strong statement last time: human beings are more important than finance.
It is high time to reaffirm it
In any case, we will remain very vigilant and will not fail to renew our call in a constructive manner.
Six member States hold in their hands the fate of 18 million Europeans in need.
six member states that claim to be doing no more and no less than legalism
Jurisprudence is one thing, humanity and political choices are another.

There is no question of renationalizing this aid.

main recommendation of this report for greater professional mobility, greater simplification and efficiency in the procedures for recognition of professional qualifications, while ensuring patient safety and consumer protection.

It is important that each year the European Parliament reviews the work of the ACP-EU Parliamentary Assembly, to the extent that
This Assembly plays a crucial role in strengthening North-South dialogue and establishing a development policy based on partnership and exchange.
The year 2010 was a busy one for the APP, which met twice, as previously mentioned by my predecessors, in Spain and the DRC, to exchange views, among other matters
on the impact of climate change in ACP countries, the impact of the global crisis, or on media freedom and independence and food security.
However, in 2010, the APP also sent its first election observation mission for this legislative term.
I had the honor of co-chairing this mission in Burundi for the presidential elections alongside my colleague Assarid, Member of Parliament from Mali.
This election observation mission was a complete success.

We were able to meet with all the relevant political stakeholders, and thanks to the dual origin of this delegation—both ACP and European—we embodied a legitimacy distinct from that of purely European or African missions.
I invite the ACP Secretariat to renew this experience, which enables practical implementation of solidarity and the special relations linking the European Union to the ACP countries.
I would like to thank Mr. Dardenne for his report.
Certainly, the crisis is economic and financial, but above all, for our fellow citizens, it is a social crisis.
Reducing by 20 million the number of people in precarious situations by 2020 requires strong measures involving all levels of public authority.

We must more than ever uphold the commitments made by the European Union in 2010, during the European Year for Combating Poverty and Social Exclusion, and in establishing the European Platform against Poverty and Social Exclusion, in order to have a European framework for social and territorial cohesion.

We must sustain and optimize European funding, particularly the program supporting the most disadvantaged, currently maintained, strengthen social protection, and support local or national initiatives developing services for the care of children, elderly individuals, and dependent persons.
Poverty is a scourge to be fought horizontally, and it is high time to act in order to enable the most vulnerable to overcome their precarious situation.

And as political leaders, at the European, national, and local levels, we must take the necessary measures to ensure them, without discrimination, access to housing, access to healthcare, access to justice, access to education, and access to a first job with a minimum income, possibly indexed according to the poverty threshold of each Member State.
Investing in promoting innovation and social experimentation and in reducing poverty will enable us to establish a sustainable, smart, and inclusive growth strategy.
The Europe we wish for and currently need is a Europe based on solidarity.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would first like to congratulate the rapporteur, Ms. Levin, on her excellent work.
As rapporteur for opinion of the Development Committee, I am pleased that the Fisheries Committee has taken up the elements contained in our opinion, as it is crucial that the development dimension
be taken into account in measures aimed at combating illegal fishing globally, in the interest of policy coherence and close alignment with development policy.
Indeed, combating illegal fishing requires comprehensive solutions and the full implementation by all states, including developing ones.
However, it is necessary that the technical and financial capacities of states are also taken into account in the actions to be undertaken.
Indeed, one cannot impose the same constraints on developed and developing countries without accompanying measures.
Small island states, for example, have very limited capabilities.
They must therefore be supported in their efforts to combat illegal fishing.
And this is why the Union will need to increase the financial allocation for the fishing sector when concluding agreements in this area with developing countries.

Moreover, it is crucial that measures aimed at combating illegal fishing be implemented, as this practice undermines the development of countries by reducing the financial resources these nations could otherwise derive from the economic activity of fishing.
Otherwise, these resources are necessary for developing countries to implement social, economic, and education policies.

Finally, it is essential that the European Union promote, in implementing its measures to combat illegal fishing in developing countries, the involvement of civil society and businesses alongside the recipient states.
Adopting a holistic approach is necessary to combat this scourge of illegal fishing. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to say that largely thanks to these outermost regions, the European Union is the world's leading maritime power.
This represents a major advantage for Europe. Therefore, the development and implementation of a comprehensive policy on seas, oceans, and seabed resources are essential for the future.
But I fear that with 20 or 50 million euros, it may not be enough to achieve this major challenge.

Combating illegal fishing is part of this broader policy, and it is urgent at the European level to find an appropriate solution to this issue, particularly within the framework of international cooperation.
I would also like to point out that the maritime zones of Guadeloupe, French Guiana, Martinique, Mayotte, and Réunion are regularly violated and plundered.
Since the report does not address this issue, I take this opportunity to draw the attention of my colleagues and the Commission to this particular matter.
Thank you, Madam President. I would first like to welcome the Polish Presidency, which has demonstrated dynamism and determination.

That said, we clearly see that today financial stability has become the paramount priority, and the pressures and tensions exerted from outside the euro area, as well as those within it, are undermining our credibility and eroding confidence.
And to hope for the return of confidence, it is necessary to restore financial stability, halt the alarming rise of national deficits and sovereign debts.
Financial stability has truly become the top priority, an urgent obligation for everyone.

Our democracies have in fact all committed themselves to very courageous programs aimed at restoring the health of their public finances and reducing their debts. But financial stability is also a prerequisite for any recovery in growth.
but whoever speaks of solidarity, of financial stability, and budgetary discipline—naturally going hand in hand—must also speak of solidarity.
We believe it is important to ensure this dual movement toward financial stability, budgetary discipline, and solidarity.
A final word on democracy.
Beware of disempowering our democratic institutions.

Rather than considering recourse to a judicial mechanism to impose sanctions in cases of budgetary deviation, let us instead focus on mechanisms of oversight and sanctions exercised by democratic institutions.

To be fully French, I am not the spokesperson for the French Minister of Foreign Affairs. And quite frankly, even though I belong to the same political party as him, I am not constantly, I would say, in a position to relay the minister's statements.

to safeguard this shared project and the preservation of the European Union. And in this effort to safeguard the euro, we must of course look to the future, consider the protection of younger generations, and ensure the preservation of peace on our continent.

development that has led to accompanying measures in the banana sector for ACP exporting countries following the reduction of their tariff preference, the ACP Banana Agreement.

At the risk of offending those of you who are most committed to federalism, I believe that a defense policy is eminently a national policy.

Defense is guaranteed in Washington, as the country prioritizes acquiring American equipment, Europe being unable, in this area as in so many others, to enforce community preference.

For thirty years, and even more so since the disappearance of the Soviet threat, defense budgets have been the first to be sacrificed on the altar of fiscal austerity, to the extent that most of our military tools have become

deficient or outdated, and all in the name of purely financial considerations, each one waiting for the neighbor or the supposed American big brother to make the efforts they themselves are no longer willing to make

Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to express my support for the French group's proposal within the PPE regarding the European Economic Stability, Coordination, and Governance Treaty, and to endorse the main conclusions reached by the Council.

but a treaty, that is to say, a set of rules and mutual commitments, can provide trust and accountability.

Ms. Ashton, good morning. Madagascar is one of the poorest countries in the world and is currently experiencing a political crisis, the resolution of which will depend on the implementation of the roadmap leading up to the presidential and legislative elections.
The growth of this country is negative.
Under normal circumstances, our contribution amounts to approximately 50% of this country's budget.
The European Union has rightly suspended its budgetary support for the 9th and 10th EFFs, but not for the MDGs.
Meanwhile, the population suffers, and businesses established in Madagascar are sometimes compelled to contribute, despite themselves.

While supporting your position, we urge you to exert your full influence with the SADEC to resolve as soon as possible the complex legal and judicial situation regarding the return of the former president, which constitutes one of the most sensitive aspects of implementing this roadmap.

I would appreciate your views on this matter, and I would also like to inform you that the newly established European Union-Madagascar Friendship Group at the European Parliament is available to work with you and the Commission in the interest of the Malagasy people and in defense of the values of our Union.

Mr. President, dear colleague, I would like to commend the rapporteur, Mr. Iturguise, for the quality of his work, for the lengthy negotiation process he had to undertake, and for the patience he demonstrated.

We have finally reached the vote on this report, which is extremely important, as it enables us to bring to this Chamber, before the Commission and the Council, the difficulties and concerns of our fellow citizens.
Here is further evidence, if any were needed, of the effectiveness of the right of petition in Europe.
Petitions concerning the environment are predominant, and waste represents a significant concern for our fellow citizens.
This topic must address a social issue.
We consume recklessly.
We are only now beginning to realize that what we do not consume, we discard.
This awareness must be firmly reinforced among citizens, that is clear.
but this report also serves as a reminder to the member states.
It is nonetheless concerning to see that states appear to be losing control of the situation.
Only six member states have transposed the waste framework directive on time.
The number of complaints received on this matter sometimes reveals a lack of transparency in the selection of landfill locations.
Finally, and this is the most alarming, organized crime appears to be doing as it pleases.
A market worth 75 billion euros inevitably attracts ambitions.
States must send a strong signal
Sanctions against companies that disclose public contracts for personal gain must be increased.
This report serves as a warning to States, but the European Commission must also fulfill its role by ensuring the proper implementation of waste directives.
This file must be made visible, but also credible.
If no one plays by the rules, we will continue to waste time and money on verification and cross-verification procedures that could drag on for years.
Let us heed the petitioners' message and put an end to this waste more quickly.
The Commissioner, Mr. Rapporteur, I would first like to congratulate Mr. Nicholson for the courage he has shown in facing the Council and the Commission.

This agreement reminds me of that well-known illusion told by children about a paradise where vegetarian lions would graze alongside sheep.

Do we want this for the European level?

Madam Commissioner, the data protection standards of third countries still differ significantly from European standards.
It is nevertheless our duty to ensure that our minimum European standards of protection, which we uphold, are respected and guaranteed to European citizens.
It is essential that personal data be processed fairly and collected for specified and legitimate purposes.

European citizens must be ensured the right of access to and rectification of personal data concerning them, as well as the right to bring a judicial remedy in the event of a violation of their rights.
Action must be taken to allow European and international standards to coexist and to resolve a potentially harmful legal conflict for European citizens.
European legislation alone is not sufficient.
It must be intelligently articulated and coexist with international standards, as we cannot renounce our duties and commitments to European citizens.
This situation echoes other debates taking place within this assembly.
It reminds us of the relevance of initiatives such as the establishment of a European programme for monitoring terrorist financing,

Such a system would enable us to have at our disposal a tool that has proven its effectiveness in the United States, while fully respecting domestic law and thus ensuring the protection of personal data of European citizens.
European institutions must continue their commitment in this regard and fully uphold their protection standards. Thank you.
Thank you, Mr. President. First of all, thank you to the rapporteurs for their work.

In response to the crisis, the European Union has spent over a year strengthening its economic governance to prevent macroeconomic imbalances and excessive budgetary deficits threatening the European economy. Yet this has not prevented either social exclusion or the rise in unemployment.
It is time—indeed, it has become urgent—that the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy become an absolute priority for the European Commission and for Member States, in the context of growth.

Each state will need to implement a national employment plan, meaning that emphasis should be placed on numerous issues such as, in no particular order, training, vocational reorientation, microcredit, apprenticeships, paid internships, and so on.

Moreover, and as Commissioner Andor reminded us just now, we have 23 million unemployed people in Europe, as well as 23 million small and medium-sized enterprises and 23 billion euros in European funds dedicated to employment that remain unused.
We could almost make it a 23-23 strategy.
Not to mention the 60 billion euros in unallocated cohesion funds.
In Denmark and the Netherlands, social initiatives have been implemented and proven effective, based on flexi-security,
Let us draw inspiration from this.

This also means that our social policy as a whole must be better adapted, with more favorable social incentives than those linked to unemployment, and active support for all socially responsible enterprises.
We had poverty reduction targets to achieve by 2020, yet we already know they will not be met due to the lack of strong and appropriate measures taken by the member states.

Our role as parliamentarians must therefore be fully exercised, and it is our responsibility to demand that the same energy devoted to implementing strong economic governance measures also be directed toward ambitious, effective, and binding social governance measures.
Thank you, Madam President. Undoubtedly, the cause of women owes much to Europe.
The principle of gender equality was already enshrined in the Treaty of Rome and has been consistently reaffirmed ever since in numerous treaties.
Gender equality is truly a fundamental value of our European project.
As a result, Europe has consistently pursued gender equality and the advancement of women with great determination.
Little by little, progress was made.
Numerous legislative instruments have been adopted and implemented, along with guidelines.
Here, within our Parliament itself, for many years there has been an extremely active and vigilant Committee on Women's Rights and Gender Equality.
An European Institute for Gender Equality has been established
Programs are being implemented, and financial resources are being approved year after year.
And more recently, on March 2, we had the opportunity to celebrate, for its second edition, European Day for Equal Treatment and Pay Equality, Equal Payers.
A European equal payment day. Undoubtedly, the legislative framework exists. European intent has been concretely realized on multiple occasions.
Yet it must be acknowledged that the struggle remains ever relevant—the struggle for gender equality, and in particular for equal pay.
On average, across all countries of the European Union, there is always a wage gap of approximately 17%.
At equal work, women still earn 17% less than men.
This is of course entirely unacceptable.
The Women's Committee of the Parliament is currently working on a draft resolution calling on the European Commission to review the 2006 directive on equality...
which is one of the most pressing struggles and a central concern in the worries and demands of women across European societies.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to thank the rapporteur and our assistants for the work they have carried out.

citizen participation, healthcare abroad, twinning initiatives, minority rights, etc. We still struggle too much to fully benefit from the capabilities offered by Europe.

Thank you, Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues. I would first like to firmly dispel the unfounded claims circulating in France about 500,000 to 1 million counterfeit biometric passports being in circulation in our country.
France takes the risks of fraud related to identity documents very seriously.
She does not take offenses in this area lightly.
At the initiative of its government, identity theft is a criminal offense punishable by one year in prison and a fine of 15,000 euros.
The conditions for issuing identity documents have been simplified, enabling prefectures to allocate additional resources to combat this fraud.
Thus, in each prefecture, a fraud liaison officer is designated.
Moreover, the measures securing the issuance of identity documents have been strengthened.
For example, civil status data provided may be verified across administrative services, as much as possible through digital means, in order to minimize the risk of falsification.
Certain birth certificates are also issued on secure paper.
The same rigor applies to proof of address documents.
The Ministry of the Interior has committed to enhancing the security of proof of address documents by incorporating a barcode.

The standard procedure for processing a title application includes authorized access to relevant files, comparison of all documents submitted in previous title applications, and, if necessary, authentication of supporting documents.

The security of title issuance is further ensured by the applicant's two personal appearances: once at the time of application submission, and again at the time of title collection, along with the taking of two fingerprints.
And this does not even mention the enhanced verification procedures for the renewal of lost or stolen documents, in comparison with data retained by prefectures.
Furthermore, France notifies international cooperation bodies, and in particular Interpol, of passports that have been issued, lost, or stolen, so that they are not used for fraudulent purposes.
Dear colleagues, France, like the rest of the European Union, is committed

Mr. President, Commissioner, first of all, a observation. Our fellow citizens increasingly wish to find and consume foods produced locally and in season, for reasons of health and quality in particular.
We must find ways to enable farmers to respond effectively and promptly to this growing demand.

We must support the establishment of local infrastructure for producers, such as maintaining local slaughterhouses, which, while ensuring rigorous sanitary control, enable the development of local short supply chains and strengthen direct sales.
Support for local agriculture and direct sales must help producers market their products and make them known to consumers.
Strengthening these direct relationships will enable farmers to retain a larger share of the added value generated on their farms.

Direct sales within the farm operation should be encouraged and facilitated, and collective initiatives by farmers who wish to establish shared sales points should be supported, enabling local consumers to access a broader range of products.

The European Commission should facilitate the emergence, sustainability, and development of direct delivery systems to consumers, such as AMAPs in France, which enable nearly 3,000 farmers to supply 60,000 households.

Finally, Member States must be able to add additional criteria, in particular regarding farm size, production volume, or the traditional nature of the product, in order to define local agriculture. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues. I welcome the fact that this corrective budget concerning the funding of ITER, which was adopted by a large majority in the Budgets Committee on 29 March last.
By funding this additional cost, we avoid discarding a strategic fundamental research project in which the European Union plays a leading role, and we uphold our international commitments.
We recall that none of the seven Euratom community partners has challenged its overall contribution to the project.
Japan, despite the Fukushima tragedy, has decided to strictly honor its budgetary commitments to ITER.
Without this agreement, hundreds of researchers would have had to suspend their work, and the ITER project would have suffered detrimental delays.
Today, a thousand people work for ITER on a full-time basis.
From 2015 until 2017–2018, the construction phase of the buildings and assembly of the machine could involve up to 4,000 to 5,000 people at this site.
To conclude, Mr. President, ITER is a source of pride for Europe and a symbol of our excellence.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rapporteur, thank you for the work we have accomplished together.
The European Union needs a manifesto, a shared political vision on the major challenges related to the environment by 2020 and beyond.
This is precisely the value of launching a seventh environmental action programme by synergizing existing financial frameworks and mobilizing new sources of funding.
This program must ensure coherence and coordination between the European Union's environmental strategies and sectoral policies, whether agriculture,
Fishing, research and innovation, health, transport, regional development, territorial planning, and urban policies, of course.
Despite the economic crisis afflicting the European Union, we must not succumb to a short-term perspective and abandon all ambition in the environmental and climate fields.
Inaction would be fatal.
The stakes are too high, particularly with regard to the efficient use of our resources and the valuation of natural capital.
We must therefore decisively invest in the sustainable economy and eco-innovation, sources of growth, competitiveness, and employment.
It is our responsibility to promote eco-design, recycling, and environmental product labeling in order to prevent waste and misguided consumption choices.
We cannot overlook this essential environmental pedagogy for European citizens.
The environmental action program is a tool for political planning, but it is also a communication instrument.

It must provide the necessary readability and predictability for national and local authorities, citizens, as well as entrepreneurs and investors, regarding the European Union's environmental choices.
European regulation must not become a punishment or a straitjacket.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, in the face of the profound transformations to which our world is currently subject, the EU must reform its Generalized System of Preferences.
Like any compromise, this one is no exception to the rule.
While satisfactory from a strictly commercial standpoint, it is far from perfect. We must keep in mind that this regulation is aimed at developing countries.
By developing its new paradigm of differentiation, the Commission states its intention to refocus its efforts in favor of the poorest countries,
This does not appear entirely tenable at first glance,
To this end, the Commission has nonetheless developed a strictly accounting vision, based solely on the criterion of GDP per capita.
One can clearly see the limitation of this approach.
What about the many middle-income developing countries whose economies are little diversified and are vulnerable to climate shocks?
The compromise reached with the Council does not address this issue, which is why I urge my colleagues to vote in favor of the three amendments to be submitted on this text.
Commissioner, the issue of the availability of public consultations across the various plans of the European Union is not new, unfortunately.

This is a crucial issue in my view. To that end, I have not only submitted numerous written questions to the European Commission since 2008, but I have also personally addressed written inquiries to Mr. Barroso.

Indeed, the Commission must comply with the principle of equal access to information for all European citizens, as reminded by the Court of First Instance of the European Communities in its judgment of 20 November 2008.
I was entirely dissatisfied with the European Commission's response, according to which public consultations do not aim to provide a fully representative picture of all opinions.
According to this response, the Commission takes into account the fact that certain positions and viewpoints may be overrepresented at the expense of others.
That is not a response.
I am questioning the criteria that would allow us to measure the overrepresentation of the European population.
If written consultations are not overrepresented due solely to availability in English, for example, they fail to achieve their objective of consulting European citizens.

Thank you, Mr. President. I would first like to commend the determination of the European Commission, and particularly your own, Mr. Commissioner, in tirelessly placing the revitalization and deepening of the internal market at the top of the European agenda.

As you reminded us, Mr. Commissioner, it has already been over two years since both the Commission and the European Parliament have been working to identify the missing links in the internal market, to pinpoint the issues and sectors where a stronger internal market is needed. A stronger internal market for greater growth, greater competitiveness, and more jobs.

On its part, the European Council also ultimately emphasized the full importance it attached to the swift and effective implementation of the twelve priority proposals for the internal market, the twelve growth levers.
Rather than constantly striving to invent new growth instruments, we stand to gain by working with the toolkit we already have.

We therefore seize the opportunity provided by the resolution to be voted on tomorrow by our Assembly to send a strong political message to the Member States: greater speed and effectiveness in transposing directives already adopted.
Greater mobilization and commitment in newly opened projects. I will mention only one, the reinforcement of worker mobility. Greater ease of movement, greater flow of traffic.
We will welcome with great interest, Mr. Commissioner, the second stage of the act for the single market that you will present after the summer.
We will demonstrate both political will and the ability to exert pressure on member states. Thank you.

Mr. President, we are currently experiencing a situation characterized by a production increase of more than 2.5%. At the same time, industrialists are lowering prices.
In Italy, Germany, England, and across all European countries, prices are decreasing.
This clearly means that prices are linked to production volume, and we must therefore organize both production volumes and prices simultaneously.
At the same time, today, producers are suffering an increase of over 44% in vegetable protein prices and a 30% rise in cereal prices.
The Parliament has already voted in favor of ensuring here that the added value is shared between producers, so that farmers are not victimized by companies and large retailers.

Therefore, I call on the Commission today, as well as on the Council, to reconsider the challenge to production control. At the same time, I make it very clear that Member States must clean up their own houses, as there are countries that have respected quota rules and others that have not.
And it is precisely this that has led to these quotas being called into question.
There is thus a dual responsibility: the Commission and the Member States.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, I am speaking on behalf of Maurice Pongard, the rapporteur for opinion of the Development Committee on Mr. Gallagher's report.
It seemed important to emphasize that the European Union, when seeking to sanction a country engaged in non-sustainable fishing practices, must take into account its level of development.

Indeed, in the name of the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, I consider it essential that measures adopted in the field of fisheries do not undermine the development plans and strategies established by the European Union in these countries.
Mr. Gallagher's draft report takes up this important principle for our committee, providing that measures must not be unjustified nor even discriminatory.

I therefore urge the European Commission, when adopting such measures, to respect the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, and to duly take into account the level of development and vulnerability of countries. Thank you.
This third reform of the common fisheries policy must not once again be carried out at the expense of fishermen's interests.

No, not all fishermen are these plunderers of the sea, the sole responsible parties for the poor health of fish stocks, as environmental organizations and the European Commission claim by lumping together all categories of fishing.

It goes without saying that fish stocks must be preserved, but we must also enable our fishermen to earn a living from their activity and safeguard the economic fabric, vitality, and cultural identity of our coastal regions.
Or the main measures of the European executive will once again aim at reducing the number of fishermen in order to achieve the mythical maximum sustainable yield.
I remind you that the French fleet has decreased by more than 50% since 1980.

The Commission notably proposes the introduction of individual transferable quotas, seen as a guarantee of industrialization and sectoral consolidation, as well as a complete ban on discharges, which professionals consider unrealistic.
This reform proposal may suit the industrial fleet, but as it stands, it would mean the programmed demise of what remains of local and artisanal fishing.

We all know here that the European Union provides more than half of the world's total public development assistance, and yet, as the 2015 deadline for the MDGs approaches, even if certain goals appear to be
Affected, we must ask ourselves how to improve our assistance, making it more consistent with our trade policy, our agricultural policy, and our foreign policy.
The future of European development policy must be based on clear, measurable commitments, established according to a short- and medium-term timetable.

The economic crisis, climate change, the rising influence of emerging countries, and land grabbing compel us to establish new priorities. Priority must be given to ensuring food security, access to energy resources, and access to healthcare.

Certainly, a European consensus exists, but we must rethink European and international environmental and social institutional systems, drawing inspiration from bodies that function effectively in decision-making, such as the WTO, for example.
Finally and above all, we must explain to our fellow citizens that the development of third countries is not merely an act of charity and solidarity, but also a source of development for ourselves.
One need only look at the markets opening up for Europeans in emerging countries—China, Brazil, and India.
2015 will also provide an opportunity to better account for the geostrategic developments of recent years, as the emerging countries of today were only recently developing nations.
Now, as indispensable economic powers, these countries must also contribute to international aid.
The European Union must be the driving force behind responsible development assistance at both the European and international levels.

The year 2015 will be more than a deadline; it will be a unique opportunity to rethink development aid, not merely as financial assistance, but as support for responsible development projects, both socially and environmentally.

I therefore join my colleagues from the other groups, as well as those from the Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions, in calling for 2015 to be designated as the European Year of Development and, above all, of responsible development.
Thank you, Mr. President. In my view, the Commission's agenda for change raises numerous questions, the main one being the practical implementation of the principle of differentiation.
The Commission wishes that, starting from 2014, the selection of countries in which the European Union will invest its development aid be based mechanically on the single criterion of GDP per capita.

Moreover, as Mrs. Ashton has reminded us, these issues are not solely accounting matters, but warrant a genuine political debate regarding the definition of our priorities in international cooperation.
Moreover, in my view, this differentiated approach is not consistent with the spirit of the Cotonou Agreement.

I am convinced that ACP countries that have achieved positive economic performance should be encouraged in their transition toward more sustainable growth. These countries should not be unfairly penalized for having succeeded in ensuring growth through discipline, sound governance, and prudent economic policies.

And there are a number of issues, and I would like to hear your thoughts on these issues, which lie at the heart of the economic and social crisis as unfortunately, we know it—tax harmonization, public services,
protector, minimum wage in Europe, establishment of enhanced cooperation, job-creating initiatives, facilitating cross-border cooperation, and developing a policy of fair exchange.
Here are the stakes, I believe, that lie before us, 20 years later.

Thank you, Mr. President. As a member of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection, I have closely followed my colleague Regina Bastos's report on the 20 main concerns of citizens and businesses.
We have debated this report, in connection with our broader discussion on the 20th anniversary of the internal market.
Twenty years already, the free movement of goods, people, services, and capital has been expected to function fully within our shared space of life, exchange, and internal market.

I have particularly been interested in the fact that among the foremost concerns expressed by our European fellow citizens are professional qualifications licences, and that we must therefore be truly proactive and particularly effective.

This is what our fellow citizens expect in order to truly establish, in the very near future, procedures for recognizing their qualifications, skills, and professional credentials that are genuinely faster, simpler, more effective, and less costly.
It is a genuine issue.

As the fictitious rapporteur for the PPE group on this issue—the modernization of these procedures—I am actively working with the other members of the IMCO committee to truly address this major challenge of mobility.
Greater mobility for a greater Europe. That is what our fellow citizens are asking for. Thank you.
This year, the Petitions Committee has acquired the right to be automatically associated with the hearings on European citizens' initiatives.
It is, for me, the clear signal of the year 2011 for this committee.
Citizens already had a body to approach in case of violation of their rights, but they can now also do so when they have proposals for improving European legislation.
I wish that the Petitions Committee could thus increasingly be regarded as the preferred point of contact for citizens within the European Parliament.
This issue of consideration is also found in the outcomes of the Committee on Petitions' positions.
Too often, we are approached by citizens facing legal entanglements on which we would like to take a stance. But while the issues are European,
Solutions should be European; we remain powerless because subsidiarity still does not align with European solutions due to a lack of national will.

I will take the challenging example of cross-border divorces. The issues are both national and European in nature when custody or visitation rights extend beyond national borders, in this case those of Germany.
Despite the obvious difficulties faced by many parents whose children are born from binational unions, we remain powerless to act while in Parliament.

My proposal that all European citizens should have the right to a national ombudsman, particularly from Germany, which submits a large number of petitions, modeled on France's Defender of Rights, has had no effect.
Yet, in the case of transnational divorces, this remains a solution that reconciles a problematic European dimension with a national resolution.
In many cases, European law appears powerless to meet citizens' expectations.
